{{Short description|Observed inability to reproduce scientific studies}}
{{use dmy dates|cs1-dates=ly|date=January 2022}}
{{cs1 config|name-list-style=vanc|display-authors=6}}
[[File:Ioannidis (2005) Why Most Published Research Findings Are False.pdf|thumb|alt=first page of research paper|Ioannidis (2005): "[[Why Most Published Research Findings Are False]]".<ref name=Ioannidis2005>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ioannidis JP | title = Why most published research findings are false | journal = PLOS Medicine | volume = 2 | issue = 8 | article-number = e124 | date = August 2005 | pmid = 16060722 | pmc = 1182327 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pmed.0020124 | doi-access = free }}</ref>]]

The '''replication crisis''', also known as the '''reproducibility''' or '''replicability crisis''', refers to widespread failures to reproduce published scientific results. Because the [[reproducibility]] of empirical results is the cornerstone of the [[scientific method]],<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = John S | title=Scientific Method | publisher=Routledge | publication-place=New York, NY | date=8 December 2017 | isbn=978-1-315-10070-8 | doi=10.4324/9781315100708 | s2cid=201781341 }}</ref> such failures undermine the credibility of theories and challenge substantial parts of scientific knowledge. 

[[Psychology]] and [[medicine]]<!-- citations under "Prevalence", subsections "In psychology" and "In medicine" --> have been focal points for replication efforts, with researchers systematically reexamining classic studies to verify their reliability and, when failures emerge, to identify the underlying causes.<ref>{{cite magazine |url=https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2010/12/13/the-truth-wears-off |title=The Truth Wears Off | vauthors = Lehrer J |magazine=The New Yorker |language=en |access-date=2020-01-30 |date=December 13, 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine |url=https://www.newyorker.com/tech/annals-of-technology/the-crisis-in-social-psychology-that-isnt |title=The Crisis in Social Psychology That Isn't | vauthors = Marcus G |magazine=The New Yorker |language=en |access-date=2020-01-30 |date=May 1, 2013}}</ref> Data strongly indicates that other [[natural science|natural]] and [[social sciences]] are also affected.<ref name="Nature 5-25-2016">{{cite journal | vauthors = Baker M | title = 1,500 scientists lift the lid on reproducibility | journal = Nature | volume = 533 | issue = 7604 | pages = 452–454 | date = May 2016 | pmid = 27225100 | doi = 10.1038/533452a | publisher = [[Springer Nature]] | type = News Feature<!-- cite journal is still the correct template, it's a News Feature article published in a journal --> | s2cid = 4460617 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2016Natur.533..452B }}{{erratum|https://www.nature.com/articles/533452a#change-history<!-- change was on 28 July 2016 -->|checked=yes}}</ref><!-- citations under "Prevalence", subsection "Overall" -->

The phrase "replication crisis" was coined in the early 2010s as part of a growing awareness of the problem.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pashler H, Harris CR | title = Is the Replicability Crisis Overblown? Three Arguments Examined | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 531–536 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168109 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612463401 | s2cid = 1342421 }}</ref> Considerations of causes and remedies have given rise to a new scientific discipline known as [[metascience]],<ref name=":19">{{cite web | vauthors = Fidler F, Wilcox J |author-link1=Fiona Fidler |title=Reproducibility of Scientific Results |url=https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/scientific-reproducibility/#MetaScieEstaMoniEvalReprCris |website=The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy |publisher=Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University |access-date=19 May 2019 |date=2018}}</ref> which uses methods of [[empirical research]] to examine empirical research practice.<ref name=":19" />

Researchers distinguish two forms of reproducibility. ''Reproducibility'' in a narrow sense refers to reexamining and validating the analysis of a set of data. The second category, ''[[Replication (statistics)|replication]]'', involves repeating an experiment or study with new, independent data to verify the original conclusions.
{{TOC limit|3<!-- 2 levels of headings + title -->}}

==Background==

=== Replication ===
[[Reproducibility|Replication]] has been called "the cornerstone of science".<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Moonesinghe R, Khoury MJ, Janssens AC | title = Most published research findings are false-but a little replication goes a long way | journal = PLOS Medicine | volume = 4 | issue = 2 | article-number = e28 | date = February 2007 | pmid = 17326704 | pmc = 1808082 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pmed.0040028 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Simons DJ | title = The Value of Direct Replication | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 9 | issue = 1 | pages = 76–80 | date = January 2014 | pmid = 26173243 | doi = 10.1177/1745691613514755 | s2cid = 1149441 }}</ref> Environmental health scientist Stefan Schmidt began a 2009 review with this description of replication:

{{blockquote|Replication is one of the central issues in any empirical science. To confirm results or hypotheses by a repetition procedure is at the basis of any scientific conception. A replication experiment to demonstrate that the same findings can be obtained in any other place by any other researcher is conceived as an operationalization of objectivity. It is the proof that the experiment reflects knowledge that can be separated from the specific circumstances (such as time, place, or persons) under which it was gained.<ref name="Schmidt 2009" />}}

But no universal definition of replication or related concepts has been agreed on.<ref name="RP:Psychology">{{cite journal | vauthors = ((Open Science Collaboration)) | title = PSYCHOLOGY. Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science | journal = Science | volume = 349 | issue = 6251 | article-number = aac4716 | date = August 2015 | pmid = 26315443 | doi = 10.1126/science.aac4716 | hdl-access = free | s2cid = 218065162 | hdl = 10722/230596 | url = https://repository.ubn.ru.nl//bitstream/handle/2066/155639/155639.pdf }}</ref><ref name="Duvendack 2017" /><ref name="Schmidt 2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Schmidt S | title=Shall we Really do it Again? The Powerful Concept of Replication is Neglected in the Social Sciences | journal=Review of General Psychology | publisher=SAGE Publications | volume=13 | issue=2 | year=2009 | issn=1089-2680 | doi=10.1037/a0015108 | pages=90–100| s2cid=143855611 }}</ref> Replication types include: 

# direct (repeating procedures as closely as possible),<ref name="Schmidt 2009" /><ref name="Shrout 2018">{{cite journal | vauthors = Shrout PE, Rodgers JL | title = Psychology, Science, and Knowledge Construction: Broadening Perspectives from the Replication Crisis | journal = Annual Review of Psychology | volume = 69 | issue = 1 | pages = 487–510 | date = January 2018 | pmid = 29300688 | doi = 10.1146/annurev-psych-122216-011845 | publisher = Annual Reviews | author-link2 = Joseph Lee Rodgers | s2cid = 19593610 }}</ref>
# systematic (repeating with intentional changes),<ref name="Shrout 2018" /> and
# conceptual (testing hypotheses using different procedures to assess generalizability).<ref name="Schmidt 2009" /><ref name="Shrout 2018" />
''Reproducibility'' can also be distinguished from ''replication'', as referring to reproducing the same results using the same data set. Reproducibility of this type is why many researchers make their data available to others for testing.<ref name="Stanley_2018">{{cite journal | vauthors = Stanley TD, Carter EC, Doucouliagos H | title = What meta-analyses reveal about the replicability of psychological research | journal = Psychological Bulletin | volume = 144 | issue = 12 | pages = 1325–1346 | date = December 2018 | pmid = 30321017 | doi = 10.1037/bul0000169 | s2cid = 51951232 }}</ref>

Replication failures do not indicate that affected fields lack scientific rigor.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.slate.com/articles/health_and_science/science/2014/07/replication_controversy_in_psychology_bullying_file_drawer_effect_blog_posts.single.html |title=Why Psychologists' Food Fight Matters | vauthors = Meyer C, Chabris C |date=31 July 2014 |website=Slate}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/science-isnt-broken/ |title=Science Isn't Broken | vauthors = Aschwanden C |date=19 August 2015 |website=FiveThirtyEight |language=en-US |access-date=2020-01-30}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/psychology-is-starting-to-deal-with-its-replication-problem/ |title=Psychology Is Starting To Deal With Its Replication Problem | vauthors = Aschwanden C |date=27 August 2015 |website=FiveThirtyEight |language=en-US |access-date=2020-01-30}}</ref> Rather, they reflect the normal operation of science—a mechanism by which unsupported hypotheses are eliminated,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/science/head-quarters/2014/may/28/psychology-replication-drive-methods-bullying |title=Psychology's replication drive: it's not about you | vauthors = Etchells P |date=28 May 2014 |newspaper=The Guardian}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wagenmakers EJ, Wetzels R, Borsboom D, van der Maas HL, Kievit RA | title = An Agenda for Purely Confirmatory Research | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 632–638 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168122 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612463078 | s2cid = 5096417 | doi-access =  }}</ref> but which often functions slowly and inconsistently.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ioannidis JP | title = Why Science Is Not Necessarily Self-Correcting | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 645–654 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168125 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612464056 | s2cid = 11798785 | doi-access =  }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pashler H, Harris CR | title = Is the Replicability Crisis Overblown? Three Arguments Examined | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 531–536 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168109 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612463401 | s2cid = 1342421 | doi-access =  }}</ref>

A hypothesis is generally considered supported when the results match the predicted pattern and that pattern is found to be [[statistically significant]]. Under [[null hypothesis]] assumption, results are deemed statistically significant when their probability falls below a predetermined threshold (the [[significance level]]). This generally answers the question of how unlikely such results would be by chance alone if no true effect existed in the [[statistical population]]. If the probability associated with the [[test statistic]] exceeds the chosen [[Critical value (statistics)|critical value]], the results are considered statistically significant.<ref name=":16">{{Cite journal | vauthors = [[Paul Meehl|Meehl PE]] |date=1967 |title=Theory-Testing in Psychology and Physics: A Methodological Paradox |journal=Philosophy of Science |volume=34 |issue=2 |pages=103–115 |doi=10.1086/288135 |jstor=186099 |s2cid=96422880 |issn=0031-8248}}</ref> The [[p-value]] represents the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true. The standard threshold ''p''&nbsp;&lt;&nbsp;0.05 means accepting a 5% false positive rate. Some fields use smaller p-values, such as ''p''&nbsp;&lt;&nbsp;0.01 (1% chance of a false positive) or ''p''&nbsp;&lt;&nbsp;0.001 (0.1% chance of a false positive). But a smaller chance of a false positive often requires greater sample sizes or a greater chance of a [[False Negative|false negative (a correct hypothesis being erroneously found incorrect)]]. Although ''p''-value testing is the most commonly used method, it is not the only one.

=== Statistics ===

Certain terms commonly used in discussions of the replication crisis have technically precise meanings, which are presented here.<ref name="Ioannidis2005" />

In the most common case, [[Statistical hypothesis test|'''null hypothesis testing''']], there are two hypotheses, a '''null hypothesis''' <math>H_0</math> and an '''alternative hypothesis''' <math>H_1</math>. The null hypothesis is typically of the form "X and Y are [[Independence (probability theory)|statistically independent]]". For example, the null hypothesis might be "taking drug X does ''not'' change 1-year recovery rate from disease Y", and the alternative hypothesis is that it does change.

As testing for full statistical independence is difficult, the full null hypothesis is often reduced to a ''simplified'' null hypothesis "the effect size is 0", where "'''[[effect size]]'''" is a real number that is 0 if the ''full'' null hypothesis is true, and the larger the effect size is, the more the null hypothesis is false.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kelley K, Preacher KJ | title = On effect size | journal = Psychological Methods | volume = 17 | issue = 2 | pages = 137–152 | date = June 2012 | pmid = 22545595 | doi = 10.1037/a0028086 }}</ref> For example, if X is binary, then the effect size might be defined as the change in the expectation of Y upon a change of X:<math display="block">(\text{effect size}) = \mathbb{E}[Y | X=1] - \mathbb{E}[Y | X=0] </math>Note that the effect size as defined above might be zero even if X and Y are not independent, such as when their relationship is non-linear (such as <math>Y \sim \mathcal N(0, 1+X)</math>) or when one variable affects different subgroups oppositely. Since different definitions of "effect size" capture different ways for X and Y to be dependent, there are many definitions of effect size.

In practice, effect sizes cannot be directly observed, but must be measured by [[Estimator|statistical estimators]]. For example, the above definition of effect size is often measured by [[Cohen's d]] estimator. The same effect size might have multiple estimators, as they have tradeoffs between [[Efficiency (statistics)|efficiency]], [[Bias of an estimator|bias]], [[Cramér–Rao bound|variance]], etc. This further increases the number of possible statistical quantities that can be computed on a single dataset. When an estimator for an effect size is used for statistical testing, it is called a '''[[test statistic]]'''.
[[File:Distributions of the Observed signal strength v2.svg|thumb|Illustration of the 4 possible outcomes of a null hypothesis test: false negative, true negative, false positive, true positive. In this illustration, the hypothesis test is a one-sided threshold test.]]
A null hypothesis '''test''' is a decision procedure which takes in some data, and outputs either <math>H_0</math> or <math>H_1</math>. If it outputs <math>H_1</math>, it is usually stated as "there is a statistically significant effect" or "the null hypothesis is rejected".

Often, the statistical test is a (one-sided) '''threshold test''', which is structured as follows:

# Gather data <math>D</math>.
# Compute a test statistic <math>t[D]</math> for the data.
# Compare the test statistic against a '''critical value'''/'''threshold''' <math>t_{\text{threshold}}</math>. If <math>t[D] > t_{\text{threshold}}</math>, then output <math>H_1</math>, else, output <math>H_0</math>.
A two-sided threshold test is similar, but with two thresholds, such that it outputs <math>H_1</math> if either <math>t[D] < t_{\text{threshold}}^- </math> or <math>t[D] > t_{\text{threshold}}^+ </math>

There are 4 possible outcomes of a null hypothesis test: false negative, true negative, false positive, true positive. A false negative means that <math>H_0</math> is true, but the test outcome is <math>H_1</math>; a true negative means that <math>H_0</math> is true, and the test outcome is <math>H_0</math>, etc.
{| class="wikitable"
!
!Probability to reject <math>H_0</math>
!Probability to not reject <math>H_0</math>
|-
|If <math>H_0</math> is True
|α
|1-α
|-
|If <math>H_1</math> is True
|1-β (power)
|β
|}
[[File:Interaction between sample size, effect size, and statistical power.svg|thumb|Interaction between sample size, effect size, and statistical power. Distributions of sample means under the null (θ=0) and alternative hypotheses are shown. The shaded red area represents significance (α), held constant at 0.05, while the shaded green area represents statistical power (1-β). As the sample size increases, the distributions narrow, leading to clearer separation between the hypotheses and higher power. Similarly, a larger effect size increases the distance between the distributions, resulting in greater power.]]
[[Statistical significance|'''Significance level''']], false positive rate, or the alpha level, is the probability of finding the alternative to be true when the null hypothesis is true:<math display="block">(\text{significance}) := \alpha := Pr(\text{find } H_1 | H_0)</math>For example, when the test is a one-sided threshold test, then <math>\alpha = Pr_{D \sim H_0}(t[D] > t_{\text{threshold}})</math> where <math>D\sim H_0</math> means "the data is sampled from <math>H_0</math>".

'''[[Power (statistics)|Statistical power]]''', true positive rate, is the probability of finding the alternative to be true when the alternative hypothesis is true:<math display="block">(\text{power}) := 1-\beta := Pr(\text{find } H_1 | H_1) </math>where <math>\beta</math> is also called the false negative rate. For example, when the test is a one-sided threshold test, then <math>1-\beta = Pr_{D \sim H_1}(t[D] > t_{\text{threshold}})</math>.

Given a statistical test and a data set <math>D</math>, the corresponding '''[[p-value]]''' is the probability that the test statistic is at least as extreme, conditional on <math>H_0</math>. For example, for a one-sided threshold test, <math display="block">p[D] = Pr_{D'\sim H_0}(t[D'] > t[D])</math>If the null hypothesis is true, then the p-value is distributed uniformly on <math>[0, 1]</math>. Otherwise, it is typically peaked at <math>p = 0.0</math> and roughly exponential, though the precise shape of the p-value distribution depends on what the alternative hypothesis is.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Simonsohn U, Nelson LD, Simmons JP | title = p-Curve and Effect Size: Correcting for Publication Bias Using Only Significant Results | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 9 | issue = 6 | pages = 666–681 | date = November 2014 | pmid = 26186117 | doi = 10.1177/1745691614553988 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Simonsohn U, Nelson LD, Simmons JP | title = P-curve: a key to the file-drawer | journal = Journal of Experimental Psychology: General | volume = 143 | issue = 2 | pages = 534–547 | date = April 2014 | pmid = 23855496 | doi = 10.1037/a0033242 }}</ref>

Because the p-values are distributed uniformly on <math>[0, 1]</math> under the null hypothesis, researchers can set any significance level <math>\alpha</math> by computing the p-value, then output <math>H_1</math> if <math>p[D] < \alpha</math>. This is usually stated as "the null hypothesis is rejected at significance level <math>\alpha</math>", or "<math>H_1 \; (p < \alpha)</math>", such as "smoking is correlated with cancer (p < 0.001)".

===History===
The replication crisis dates to a number of events in the early 2010s. Felipe Romero identified four precursors to the crisis:<ref name="Romero_2019">{{Cite journal | vauthors = Romero F |date=November 2019 |title=Philosophy of science and the replicability crisis |journal=Philosophy Compass |language=en |volume=14 |issue=11 |article-number=e12633 |doi=10.1111/phc3.12633 |s2cid=202261836 |issn=1747-9991|doi-access=free }}</ref>

* '''Social priming failures''': In the early 2010s, two direct replication attempts failed to reproduce<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Doyen S, Klein O, Pichon CL, Cleeremans A | title = Behavioral priming: it's all in the mind, but whose mind? | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 7 | issue = 1 | article-number = e29081 | date = 2012-01-18 | pmid = 22279526 | pmc = 3261136 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0029081 | veditors = Lauwereyns J | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2012PLoSO...729081D }}</ref> results from social psychologist [[John Bargh]]'s much-cited "elderly-walking" study (originally published in 1996).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bargh JA, Chen M, Burrows L | title = Automaticity of social behavior: direct effects of trait construct and stereotype-activation on action | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 71 | issue = 2 | pages = 230–244 | date = August 1996 | pmid = 8765481 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.71.2.230 | bibcode = 1996JPSP...71..230B | s2cid = 6654763 }}</ref> This experiment was part of a series of three studies that had been widely cited throughout the years, was regularly taught in university courses, and had inspired many conceptual replications. These replication failures triggered intense disagreement between replication researchers and the original authors.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2012-03-10 |title=A failed replication draws a scathing personal attack from a psychology professor |url=https://www.nationalgeographic.com/science/article/failed-replication-bargh-psychology-study-doyen |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210225023644/https://www.nationalgeographic.com/science/article/failed-replication-bargh-psychology-study-doyen |archive-date=25 February 2021 |access-date=2023-07-04 |website= National Geographic | vauthors= Yong E| url-access= subscription|language=en}}</ref> Notably, many of the conceptual replications of the original studies also failed to replicate in subsequent direct replications.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pashler H, Coburn N, Harris CR | title = Priming of social distance? Failure to replicate effects on social and food judgments | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 7 | issue = 8 | article-number = e42510 | date = 2012-08-29 | pmid = 22952597 | pmc = 3430642 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0042510 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2012PLoSO...742510P }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Harris CR, Coburn N, Rohrer D, Pashler H | title = Two failures to replicate high-performance-goal priming effects | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 8 | issue = 8 | article-number = e72467 | date = 2013-08-16 | pmid = 23977304 | pmc = 3745413 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0072467 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2013PLoSO...872467H }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Shanks DR, Newell BR, Lee EH, Balakrishnan D, Ekelund L, Cenac Z, Kavvadia F, Moore C | title = Priming intelligent behavior: an elusive phenomenon | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 8 | issue = 4 | article-number = e56515 | date = 2013-04-24 | pmid = 23637732 | pmc = 3634790 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0056515 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2013PLoSO...856515S }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Klein RA, Ratliff KA, Vianello M, Adams RB, Bahník Š, Bernstein MJ, Bocian K, Brandt MJ, Brooks B, Brumbaugh CC, Cemalcilar Z, Chandler J, Cheong W, Davis WE, Devos T |date=May 2014 |title=Investigating Variation in Replicability |journal=Social Psychology |volume=45 |issue=3 |pages=142–152 |doi=10.1027/1864-9335/a000178 |issn=1864-9335|doi-access=free |hdl=2066/131506 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
* '''Experiments on extrasensory perception:''' Social psychologist [[Daryl Bem]] conducted a series of experiments supposedly providing evidence for the controversial phenomenon of [[extrasensory perception]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bem DJ | title = Feeling the future: experimental evidence for anomalous retroactive influences on cognition and affect | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 100 | issue = 3 | pages = 407–425 | date = March 2011 | pmid = 21280961 | doi = 10.1037/a0021524 | s2cid = 1961013 }}</ref> Bem faced substantial criticism of his study's methodology. Reanalysis of his data found no evidence for extrasensory perception.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wagenmakers EJ, Wetzels R, Borsboom D, van der Maas HL | title = Why psychologists must change the way they analyze their data: the case of psi: comment on Bem (2011) | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 100 | issue = 3 | pages = 426–432 | date = March 2011 | pmid = 21280965 | doi = 10.1037/a0022790 }}</ref> The experiment also failed to replicate in subsequent direct replications.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Galak J, LeBoeuf RA, Nelson LD, Simmons JP | title = Correcting the past: failures to replicate ψ | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 103 | issue = 6 | pages = 933–948 | date = December 2012 | pmid = 22924750 | doi = 10.1037/a0029709 }}</ref> According to Romero, what the community found particularly upsetting was that many of the flawed procedures and statistical tools used in Bem's studies were part of common research practice in psychology.
* '''Biomedical replication failures:''' Scientists from biotech companies [[Amgen]] and [[Bayer|Bayer Healthcare]] reported alarmingly low replication rates (11–20%) of landmark findings in preclinical oncological research.<ref name="Begley-Lee2012"/>
* '''P-hacking studies and questionable research practices''': Since the late 2000s, a number of studies in [[metascience]] showed how commonly adopted practices in many scientific fields, such as exploiting the flexibility of the process of data collection and reporting, could greatly increase the probability of false positive results.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ioannidis JP | title = Why most discovered true associations are inflated | language = en-US | journal = Epidemiology | volume = 19 | issue = 5 | pages = 640–648 | date = September 2008 | pmid = 18633328 | doi = 10.1097/EDE.0b013e31818131e7 | s2cid = 15440816 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name="John 2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = John LK, Loewenstein G, Prelec D | title = Measuring the prevalence of questionable research practices with incentives for truth telling | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 23 | issue = 5 | pages = 524–532 | date = May 2012 | pmid = 22508865 | doi = 10.1177/0956797611430953 | s2cid = 8400625 | author-link2 = George Loewenstein | author-link3 = Drazen Prelec }}</ref><ref name="Simmons et al. (2011)">{{cite journal | vauthors = Simmons JP, Nelson LD, Simonsohn U | title = False-positive psychology: undisclosed flexibility in data collection and analysis allows presenting anything as significant | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 22 | issue = 11 | pages = 1359–1366 | date = November 2011 | pmid = 22006061 | doi = 10.1177/0956797611417632 | s2cid = 13802986 | doi-access =  }}</ref> These studies suggested how a significant proportion of published literature in several scientific fields could be nonreplicable research.

This series of events generated a great deal of skepticism about the validity of existing research in light of widespread methodological flaws and failures to replicate findings. This led prominent scholars to declare a "crisis of confidence" in psychology and other fields,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pashler H, Wagenmakers EJ | title = Editors' Introduction to the Special Section on Replicability in Psychological Science: A Crisis of Confidence? | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 528–530 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168108 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612465253 | s2cid = 26361121 }}</ref> and the ensuing situation came to be known as the "replication crisis".

Although the beginning of the replication crisis can be traced to the early 2010s, some authors point out that concerns about replicability and research practices in the social sciences had been expressed much earlier. Romero notes that authors voiced concerns about the lack of direct replications in psychological research in the late 1960s and early 1970s.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Ahlgren A |date=April 1969 |title=A modest proposal for encouraging replication. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/h0037798 |journal=American Psychologist |language=en |volume=24 |issue=4 |page=471 |doi=10.1037/h0037798 |issn=1935-990X}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Smith NC |date=October 1970 |title=Replication studies: A neglected aspect of psychological research. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/h0029774 |journal=American Psychologist |language=en |volume=25 |issue=10 |pages=970–975 |doi=10.1037/h0029774 |issn=1935-990X}}</ref> He also writes that certain studies in the 1990s were already reporting that journal editors and reviewers are generally biased against publishing replication studies.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors =  Neuliep JW, Crandall R |date=1993 |title=Reviewer bias against replication research |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/1292304227 |journal=Journal of Social Behavior and Personality |volume=8 |issue=6 |pages=21–29 |id={{ProQuest|1292304227}} }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors =  Neuliep JW, Crandall R |date=1990 |title=Editorial bias against replication research |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/1292299467  |journal=Journal of Social Behavior and Personality |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=85–90 |id={{ProQuest|1292299467}} }}</ref>

In the social sciences, the blog [[Data Colada]] (whose three authors coined the term "[[P-Hacking|p-hacking]]" in a 2014 paper) has been credited with contributing to the start of the replication crisis.<ref name=":22">{{Cite magazine | vauthors = Lewis-Kraus G |date=2023-09-30 |title=They Studied Dishonesty. Was Their Work a Lie? |language=en-US |magazine=The New Yorker |url=https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2023/10/09/they-studied-dishonesty-was-their-work-a-lie |access-date=2023-10-01 |issn=0028-792X}}</ref><ref name=":32">{{Cite web | vauthors = Subbaraman N |date=2023-09-24 |title=The Band of Debunkers Busting Bad Scientists |url=https://www.wsj.com/science/data-colada-debunk-stanford-president-research-14664f3 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230924094046/https://www.wsj.com/science/data-colada-debunk-stanford-president-research-14664f3 |archive-date=2023-09-24 |access-date=2023-10-08 |website=[[Wall Street Journal]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |title=  P-curve: A key to the file-drawer|journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General |date=2014 |volume=143 |issue=2 |pages=534–547 |url=https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2013-25331-001 |doi=10.1037/a0033242 |pmid=23855496 | last1=Simonsohn |first1=Uri |last2=Nelson |first2=Leif D. |last3=Simmons |first3=Joseph P. }}</ref>

[[University of Virginia]] professor and cognitive psychologist [[Barbara Spellman]] has written that many criticisms of research practices and concerns about replicability of research are not new.<ref name=":1">{{cite journal | last1 = Spellman |first1 = BA | title = A Short (Personal) Future History of Revolution 2.0 | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 10 | issue = 6 | pages = 886–899 | date = November 2015 | pmid = 26581743 | doi = 10.1177/1745691615609918 | s2cid = 206778431 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name="Spellman2012">{{cite journal |last=Spellman |first=Barbara A. |title=Introduction to the special section on replicability in psychological research: Why, when, and how |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |year=2012 |volume=7 |issue=6 |pages=537–541 |doi=10.1177/1745691612465253}}</ref> She reports that between the late 1950s and the 1990s, scholars were already expressing concerns about a possible crisis of replication,<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |date=January 1976 | veditors = Greenwald AG |title=An editorial. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/h0078635 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=33 |issue=1 |pages=1–7 |doi=10.1037/h0078635 |issn=1939-1315}}</ref> a suspiciously high rate of positive findings,<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Sterling TD |date=1959 |title=Publication Decisions and Their Possible Effects on Inferences Drawn from Tests of Significance--Or Vice Versa |journal=Journal of the American Statistical Association |volume=54 |issue=285 |pages=30–34 |doi=10.2307/2282137 |jstor=2282137 |issn=0162-1459}}</ref> questionable research practices,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mills JL | title = Data torturing | journal = The New England Journal of Medicine | volume = 329 | issue = 16 | pages = 1196–1199 | date = October 1993 | pmid = 8166792 | doi = 10.1056/NEJM199310143291613 }}</ref> the effects of publication bias,<ref name=":10">{{Cite journal | vauthors = Rosenthal R |date=May 1979 |title=The file drawer problem and tolerance for null results. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/0033-2909.86.3.638 |journal=Psychological Bulletin |language=en |volume=86 |issue=3 |pages=638–641 |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.86.3.638 |s2cid=36070395 |issn=1939-1455}}</ref> issues with statistical power,<ref name=":13">{{cite journal | vauthors = [[Jacob Cohen (statistician)|Cohen J]] | title = The statistical power of abnormal-social psychological research: a review | journal = Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology | volume = 65 | pages = 145–153 | date = September 1962 | pmid = 13880271 | doi = 10.1037/h0045186 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Sedlmeier P, Gigerenzer G |date=March 1989 |title=Do studies of statistical power have an effect on the power of studies? |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/0033-2909.105.2.309 |journal=Psychological Bulletin |language=en |volume=105 |issue=2 |pages=309–316 |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.105.2.309 |hdl=21.11116/0000-0000-B883-C |issn=1939-1455|hdl-access=free }}</ref> and bad standards of reporting.<ref name=":2" />

Spellman also identifies reasons that the reiteration of these criticisms and concerns in recent years led to a full-blown crisis and challenges to the status quo. First, technological improvements facilitated conducting and disseminating replication studies, and analyzing large swaths of literature for systemic problems. Second, the research community's increasing size and diversity made the work of established members more easily scrutinized by other community members unfamiliar with them. According to Spellman, these factors, coupled with increasingly limited resources and misaligned incentives for doing scientific work, led to a crisis in psychology and other fields.<ref name=":1" />

According to [[Andrew Gelman]],<ref>{{Cite web | vauthors = Gelman A |date=September 21, 2016 |title=What has happened down here is the winds have changed |url=https://statmodeling.stat.columbia.edu/2016/09/21/what-has-happened-down-here-is-the-winds-have-changed/ |website=Statistical Modeling, Causal Inference, and Social Science}}</ref> the works of [[Paul Meehl]], [[Jacob Cohen (statistician)|Jacob Cohen]], and [[Amos Tversky|Tversky]] and [[Daniel Kahneman|Kahneman]] in the 1960s-70s were early warnings of replication crisis. In discussing the origins of the problem, Kahneman himself noted historical precedents in [[Subliminal stimuli|subliminal perception]] and [[Cognitive dissonance|dissonance reduction]] replication failures.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Yong E |date=2012-10-03 |title=Nobel laureate challenges psychologists to clean up their act |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/nature.2012.11535 |journal=Nature |language=en |doi=10.1038/nature.2012.11535 |issn=1476-4687}}</ref>

It had been repeatedly pointed out since 1962<ref name=":13" /> that most psychological studies have low power (true positive rate), but low power persisted for 50 years, indicating a structural and persistent problem in psychological research.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Vankov I, Bowers J, Munafò MR | title = On the persistence of low power in psychological science | journal = Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology | volume = 67 | issue = 5 | pages = 1037–1040 | date = May 2014 | pmid = 24528377 | pmc = 4961230 | doi = 10.1080/17470218.2014.885986 }}</ref><ref name=":14" />

==Prevalence==
=== In psychology ===
Several factors have combined to put psychology at the center of the conversation.<ref>{{cite news | vauthors = Achenbach J |title=No, science's reproducibility problem is not limited to psychology |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/speaking-of-science/wp/2015/08/28/no-sciences-reproducibility-problem-is-not-limited-to-psychology/ |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |access-date=10 September 2015}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Wiggins BJ, Christopherson C |date=2019 |title=The replication crisis in psychology: An overview for theoretical and philosophical psychology. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/teo0000137 |journal=Journal of Theoretical and Philosophical Psychology |language=en |volume=39 |issue=4 |pages=202–217 |doi=10.1037/teo0000137 |s2cid=210567289 |issn=2151-3341}}</ref> Some areas of psychology once considered solid, such as [[social priming]] and [[ego depletion]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hagger MS, Chatzisarantis NL, Alberts H, Anggono CO, Batailler C, Birt AR, Brand R, Brandt MJ, Brewer G, Bruyneel S, Calvillo DP, Campbell WK, Cannon PR, Carlucci M, Carruth NP, Cheung T, Crowell A, De Ridder DT, Dewitte S, Elson M, Evans JR, Fay BA, Fennis BM, Finley A, Francis Z, Heise E, Hoemann H, Inzlicht M, Koole SL, Koppel L, Kroese F, Lange F, Lau K, Lynch BP, Martijn C, Merckelbach H, Mills NV, Michirev A, Miyake A, Mosser AE, Muise M, Muller D, Muzi M, Nalis D, Nurwanti R, Otgaar H, Philipp MC, Primoceri P, Rentzsch K, Ringos L, Schlinkert C, Schmeichel BJ, Schoch SF, Schrama M, Schütz A, Stamos A, Tinghög G, Ullrich J, vanDellen M, Wimbarti S, Wolff W, Yusainy C, Zerhouni O, Zwienenberg M | title = A Multilab Preregistered Replication of the Ego-Depletion Effect | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 11 | issue = 4 | pages = 546–573 | date = July 2016 | pmid = 27474142 | doi = 10.1177/1745691616652873 | hdl-access = free | doi-access = free | hdl = 20.500.11937/16871 }}</ref> have come under increased scrutiny due to failed replications.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bartlett T |date=30 January 2013 |title=Power of Suggestion |url=http://chronicle.com/article/Power-of-Suggestion/136907/ |journal=The Chronicle of Higher Education}}</ref> Much of the focus has been on [[social psychology]],<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/18/magazine/when-the-revolution-came-for-amy-cuddy.html |title=When the Revolution Came for Amy Cuddy | vauthors = Dominus S |date=2017-10-18 |work=The New York Times |access-date=2017-10-19 |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> although other areas of psychology such as [[clinical psychology]],<ref name="DuncanKeller2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Duncan LE, Keller MC | title = A critical review of the first 10 years of candidate gene-by-environment interaction research in psychiatry | journal = The American Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 168 | issue = 10 | pages = 1041–1049 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 21890791 | pmc = 3222234 | doi = 10.1176/appi.ajp.2011.11020191 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Leichsenring F, Abbass A, Hilsenroth MJ, Leweke F, Luyten P, Keefe JR, Midgley N, Rabung S, Salzer S, Steinert C | title = Biases in research: risk factors for non-replicability in psychotherapy and pharmacotherapy research | journal = Psychological Medicine | volume = 47 | issue = 6 | pages = 1000–1011 | date = April 2017 | pmid = 27955715 | doi = 10.1017/S003329171600324X | author-link2 = Allan Abbass | s2cid = 1872762 | url = https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/1532689/ }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hengartner MP | title = Raising Awareness for the Replication Crisis in Clinical Psychology by Focusing on Inconsistencies in Psychotherapy Research: How Much Can We Rely on Published Findings from Efficacy Trials? | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 9 | article-number = 256 | date = February 28, 2018 | pmid = 29541051 | pmc = 5835722 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2018.00256 | publisher = [[Frontiers Media]] | doi-access = free }}</ref> [[developmental psychology]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Frank MC, Bergelson E, Bergmann C, Cristia A, Floccia C, Gervain J, Hamlin JK, Hannon EE, Kline M, Levelt C, Lew-Williams C, Nazzi T, Panneton R, Rabagliati H, Soderstrom M, Sullivan J, Waxman S, Yurovsky D | title = A Collaborative Approach to Infant Research: Promoting Reproducibility, Best Practices, and Theory-Building | journal = Infancy | volume = 22 | issue = 4 | pages = 421–435 | date = 9 March 2017 | pmid = 31772509 | pmc = 6879177 | doi = 10.1111/infa.12182 | author-link17 = Sandra Waxman | hdl = 10026.1/9942 | author-link1 = Michael C. Frank }}</ref><ref>{{cite book| vauthors = Harris JR |author-link=Judith Rich Harris|title=The Nurture Assumption: Why Children Turn Out the Way They Do|title-link=The Nurture Assumption|year=2009|orig-date=1998|place=New York|publisher=[[Free Press (publisher)|Free Press]]|edition=2nd|isbn=978-1-4391-0165-0}}</ref><ref>{{cite book| vauthors = Harris HR |author-link=Judith Rich Harris|title=No Two Alike: Human Nature and Human Individuality|title-link=No Two Alike|year=2006|place=New York|publisher=[[W. W. Norton & Company]]|isbn=978-0-393-32971-1}}</ref> and [[educational research]] have also been implicated.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tyson C |title=Failure to Replicate |journal=[[Inside Higher Ed]] |date=14 August 2014 |url= https://www.insidehighered.com/news/2014/08/14/almost-no-education-research-replicated-new-article-shows |access-date=19 December 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Makel MC, Plucker JA |author-link2=Jonathan A. Plucker |title=Facts Are More Important Than Novelty: Replication in the Education Sciences |journal=[[Educational Researcher]] |date=1 August 2014 |volume=43 |issue=6 |pages=304–316 |doi=10.3102/0013189X14545513 |s2cid=145571836 |access-date=19 December 2018 |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/stoken/rbtfl/w5mrNxPVD8zSg/full}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Kirschner PA, Sweller J, Clark RE |author-link2=John Sweller|title=Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work: An Analysis of the Failure of Constructivist, Discovery, Problem-Based, Experiential, and Inquiry-Based Teaching|journal=[[Educational Psychologist (journal)|Educational Psychologist]]|publisher=[[Routledge]]|year=2006|volume=41|issue=2|pages=75–86|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/27699659|doi=10.1207/s15326985ep4102_1|s2cid=17067829}}</ref><ref>{{cite report|title=Foundations for Success: The Final Report of the National Mathematics Advisory Panel|year=2008|publisher=[[United States Department of Education]]|pages=45–46|url=https://www2.ed.gov/about/bdscomm/list/mathpanel/report/final-report.pdf|access-date=3 November 2020|archive-date=18 January 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180118012046/https://www2.ed.gov/about/bdscomm/list/mathpanel/report/final-report.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pashler H, McDaniel M, Rohrer D, Bjork R | title = Learning Styles: Concepts and Evidence | journal = Psychological Science in the Public Interest | volume = 9 | issue = 3 | pages = 105–119 | date = December 2008 | pmid = 26162104 | doi = 10.1111/j.1539-6053.2009.01038.x | publisher = [[SAGE Publishing|SAGE Publications]] | author-link2 = Mark A. McDaniel | s2cid = 2112166 | doi-access = free | author-link4 = Robert A. Bjork | author-link1 = Hal Pashler }}</ref>

In August 2015, the first open [[Empirical research|empirical study]] of reproducibility in psychology was published, called [[Reproducibility Project|The Reproducibility Project: Psychology]]. Coordinated by psychologist [[Brian Nosek]], researchers redid 100 studies in psychological science from three high-ranking psychology journals (''[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]'', ''[[Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition]]'', and ''[[Psychological Science (journal)|Psychological Science]]''). Of 97 original studies with significant effects, 36% replicated successfully (''p'' value below 0.05), with effect sizes averaging half the original magnitude.<ref name="RP:Psychology" /> Among non-replications, 25% directly contradicted the original while 49% were inconclusive due to underpowered designs. The same paper examined the reproducibility rates and effect sizes by journal and discipline. Study replication rates were 23% for the ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 48% for ''Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition'', and 38% for ''Psychological Science''. Studies in the field of [[cognitive psychology]] had a higher replication rate (50%) than studies in the field of social psychology (25%).<ref>{{cite web| title= Summary of reproducibility rates and effect sizes for original and replication studies overall and by journal/discipline | url = https://mfr.osf.io/render?url=https://osf.io/jq7v6/?action=download%26mode=render | access-date = 16 October 2019 | work=Estimating the Reproducibility of Psychological Science | vauthors = Nosek BA, Cohoon J, Kidwell MC, Spies JR | publisher= Reproducibility Project: Psychology | date= 2018| orig-date= 2015 | type= table
}}</ref> This inconclusiveness reflected inadequate statistical power: replication samples were approximately 40% the size of the originals.<ref name=":5">{{cite journal | vauthors = Nelson LD, Simmons J, Simonsohn U | title = Psychology's Renaissance | journal = Annual Review of Psychology | volume = 69 | issue = 1 | pages = 511–534 | date = January 2018 | pmid = 29068778 | doi = 10.1146/annurev-psych-122216-011836 }}</ref>

A study published in 2018 in ''[[Nature Human Behaviour]]'' replicated 21 social and behavioral science papers from ''[[Nature (journal)|Nature]]'' and ''[[Science (journal)|Science]],'' finding that only about 62% could successfully reproduce original results.<ref>{{Cite magazine |url=https://www.wired.com/story/social-science-reproducibility/ |title=The Science Behind Social Science Gets Shaken Up—Again |magazine=Wired |language=en |access-date=2018-08-28 | vauthors = Roger A |date=2018-08-27}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Camerer CF, Dreber A, Holzmeister F, Ho TH, Huber J, Johannesson M, Kirchler M, Nave G, Nosek BA, Pfeiffer T, Altmejd A, Buttrick N, Chan T, Chen Y, Forsell E, Gampa A, Heikensten E, Hummer L, Imai T, Isaksson S, Manfredi D, Rose J, Wagenmakers EJ, Wu H | title = Evaluating the replicability of social science experiments in Nature and Science between 2010 and 2015 | journal = Nature Human Behaviour | volume = 2 | issue = 9 | pages = 637–644 | date = September 2018 | pmid = 31346273 | doi = 10.1038/s41562-018-0399-z | s2cid = 52098703 | url = https://resolver.caltech.edu/CaltechAUTHORS:20181120-072245155 | author-link1 = Colin Camerer }}</ref>

Similarly, in a study conducted under the auspices of the [[Center for Open Science]], a team of 186 researchers from 60 laboratories (representing 36 nationalities from six continents) conducted replications of 28 classic and contemporary findings in psychology.<ref name="Many Labs 2">{{cite journal | vauthors = Klein RA |title=Many Labs 2: Investigating Variation in Replicability Across Samples and Settings |journal=Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science |date=2018 |volume=1 |issue=4 |pages=443–490 |doi=10.1177/2515245918810225 |doi-access=free |hdl=1854/LU-8637133 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Witkowski" /> The study's focus was not only whether the original papers' findings replicated but also the extent to which findings varied as a function of variations in samples and contexts. Overall, 50% of findings failed to replicate despite large sample sizes. When findings did replicate, they consistently replicated across most samples. When they failed, they consistently failed across contexts, suggesting contextual sensitivity was not the primary driver of replication failures. This evidence is inconsistent with a proposed explanation that failures to replicate in psychology are likely due to changes in the sample between the original and replication study.<ref name="Witkowski">{{cite magazine | vauthors = Witkowski T |title=Is the glass half empty or half full? Latest results in the replication crisis in Psychology |magazine=[[Skeptical Inquirer]] |date=2019 |volume=43 |issue=2 |pages=5–6 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200130130328/https://forbiddenpsychology.files.wordpress.com/2019/04/pdf-article.pdf |archive-date=2020-01-30 |url=https://forbiddenpsychology.files.wordpress.com/2019/04/pdf-article.pdf}}</ref>

Results of a 2022 study suggest that many earlier [[Human brain#Research|brain]]–[[phenotype]] studies ("brain-wide association studies" (BWAS)) produced invalid conclusions as the replication of such studies requires samples from thousands of individuals due to small [[effect size]]s.<ref>{{cite news | vauthors = Richtel M |date=16 March 2022 |title=Brain-Imaging Studies Hampered by Small Data Sets, Study Finds |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/03/16/science/brain-imaging-research.html}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Marek S, Tervo-Clemmens B, Calabro FJ, Montez DF, Kay BP, Hatoum AS, Donohue MR, Foran W, Miller RL, Hendrickson TJ, Malone SM, Kandala S, Feczko E, Miranda-Dominguez O, Graham AM, Earl EA, Perrone AJ, Cordova M, Doyle O, Moore LA, Conan GM, Uriarte J, Snider K, Lynch BJ, Wilgenbusch JC, Pengo T, Tam A, Chen J, Newbold DJ, Zheng A, Seider NA, Van AN, Metoki A, Chauvin RJ, Laumann TO, Greene DJ, Petersen SE, Garavan H, Thompson WK, Nichols TE, Yeo BT, Barch DM, Luna B, Fair DA, Dosenbach NU | title = Reproducible brain-wide association studies require thousands of individuals | journal = Nature | volume = 603 | issue = 7902 | pages = 654–660 | date = March 2022 | pmid = 35296861 | pmc = 8991999 | doi = 10.1038/s41586-022-04492-9 | bibcode = 2022Natur.603..654M }}</ref>

=== In medicine ===
[[File:Barriers to conducting replications of experiment in cancer research.jpg|thumb|300px|alt=Graphic of results and barriers. 193 experiments were designed, 87 were initiated, and 50 were completed.|Results from The Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology suggest most studies of the [[cancer research]] sector may not be replicable.]]
Of 49 medical studies from 1990 to 2003 with more than 1000 citations, 92% found that the studied therapies were effective. Of these studies, 16% were contradicted by subsequent studies, 16% had found stronger effects than did subsequent studies, 44% were replicated, and 24% remained largely unchallenged.<ref name="pmid16014596">{{cite journal |vauthors=Ioannidis JP |title=Contradicted and initially stronger effects in highly cited clinical research |journal=JAMA |volume=294 |issue=2 |pages=218–28 |date=July 2005 |pmid=16014596 |doi=10.1001/jama.294.2.218 |url=}}</ref> A 2011 analysis by researchers with pharmaceutical company [[Bayer]] found that, at most, a quarter of Bayer's in-house findings replicated the original results.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Prinz F, Schlange T, Asadullah K | title = Believe it or not: how much can we rely on published data on potential drug targets? | journal = Nature Reviews. Drug Discovery | volume = 10 | issue = 9 | page = 712 | date = August 2011 | pmid = 21892149 | doi = 10.1038/nrd3439-c1 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2011NRvDD..10..712P }}</ref> But the analysis of Bayer's results found that the results that did replicate could often be successfully used for clinical applications.<ref name="Wheeling2017">{{cite news | vauthors = Wheeling K |date=May 12, 2016 |title=Big Pharma Reveals a Biomedical Replication Crisis |url=https://psmag.com/news/big-pharma-reveals-a-biomedical-replication-crisis |access-date=2020-01-30 |website=Pacific Standard |language=en}} Updated on 14 June 2017</ref>

In a 2012 paper, [[C. Glenn Begley]], a biotech consultant working at [[Amgen]], and Lee Ellis, a medical researcher at the University of Texas, found that only 11% of 53 pre-clinical cancer studies had replications that could confirm conclusions from the original studies.<ref name="Begley-Lee2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Begley CG, Ellis LM | title = Drug development: Raise standards for preclinical cancer research | journal = Nature | volume = 483 | issue = 7391 | pages = 531–533 | date = March 2012 | pmid = 22460880 | doi = 10.1038/483531a | type = Comment article<!-- cite journal is still the correct template, it's a comment article published in a journal --> | s2cid = 4326966 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2012Natur.483..531B }}{{Erratum|checked=yes|doi=10.1038/485041e}}</ref> In late 2021, The Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology examined 53 top papers about cancer published between 2010 and 2012 and showed that among studies that provided sufficient information to be redone, the effect sizes were 85% smaller on average than the original findings.<ref name="Haelle 2021">{{cite news | vauthors = Haelle T |author-link1=Tara Haelle |date=7 December 2021 |title=Dozens of major cancer studies can't be replicated |work=Science News |url=https://www.sciencenews.org/article/cancer-biology-studies-research-replication-reproducibility |access-date=19 January 2022}}</ref><ref name="RP:Cancer">{{cite web |title=Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology |url=https://www.cos.io/rpcb |access-date=19 January 2022 |website=www.cos.io |publisher=[[Center for Open Science]] |language=en}}</ref> A survey of cancer researchers found that half of them had been unable to reproduce a published result.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mobley A, Linder SK, Braeuer R, Ellis LM, Zwelling L | title = A survey on data reproducibility in cancer research provides insights into our limited ability to translate findings from the laboratory to the clinic | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 8 | issue = 5 | article-number = e63221 | year = 2013 | pmid = 23691000 | pmc = 3655010 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0063221 | veditors = Arakawa H | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2013PLoSO...863221M }}</ref> Another report estimated that almost half of randomized controlled trials contained flawed data (based on the analysis of anonymized individual participant data (IPD) from more than 150 trials).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Van Noorden R | title = Medicine is plagued by untrustworthy clinical trials. How many studies are faked or flawed? | journal = Nature | volume = 619 | issue = 7970 | pages = 454–458 | date = July 2023 | pmid = 37464079 | doi = 10.1038/d41586-023-02299-w | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2023Natur.619..454V }}</ref>

=== In other disciplines ===

==== In nutrition science ====
In nutrition science, for most food ingredients, there were studies that found that the ingredient has an effect on cancer risk. Specifically, out of a random sample of 50 ingredients from a cookbook, 80% had articles reporting on their cancer risk. Statistical significance decreased for meta-analyses.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schoenfeld |first1=Jonathan D. |last2=Ioannidis |first2=John P. A. |date=January 2013 |title=Is everything we eat associated with cancer? A systematic cookbook review |journal=The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition |volume=97 |issue=1 |pages=127–134 |doi=10.3945/ajcn.112.047142 |issn=1938-3207 |pmid=23193004}}</ref>

==== In economics ====
[[Economics]] has lagged behind other social sciences and psychology in its attempts to assess replication rates and increase the number of studies that attempt replication.<ref name="Duvendack 2017">{{Cite journal | vauthors = Duvendack M, Palmer-Jones R, Reed RW |date=May 2017 |title=What Is Meant by "Replication" and Why Does It Encounter Resistance in Economics? |url=https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.p20171031 |journal=American Economic Review |language=en |volume=107 |issue=5 |pages=46–51 |doi=10.1257/aer.p20171031 |issn=0002-8282}}</ref> A 2016 study in the journal ''Science'' replicated 18 [[experimental studies]] published in two leading economics journals, ''[[American Economic Review|The American Economic Review]]'' and the ''[[Quarterly Journal of Economics]]'', between 2011 and 2014. It found that approximately 61% of studies successfully replicated, though the replicated effect sizes were only 66% of the original reported effect sizes on average, suggesting that the original studies' effect sizes were inflated.<ref name="Tsui" /><ref name="Camerer">{{cite journal | vauthors = Camerer CF, Dreber A, Forsell E, Ho TH, Huber J, Johannesson M, Kirchler M, Almenberg J, Altmejd A, Chan T, Heikensten E, Holzmeister F, Imai T, Isaksson S, Nave G, Pfeiffer T, Razen M, Wu H | title = Evaluating replicability of laboratory experiments in economics | journal = Science | volume = 351 | issue = 6280 | pages = 1433–1436 | date = March 2016 | pmid = 26940865 | doi = 10.1126/science.aaf0918 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2016Sci...351.1433C }}</ref><ref name="Bohannon">{{cite journal |url=https://www.science.org/content/article/about-40-economics-experiments-fail-replication-survey-rev2 |title=About 40% of economics experiments fail replication survey | vauthors = Bohannon J |date=2016-03-03 |journal=Science |language=en |access-date=2017-10-25 | doi = 10.1126/science.aaf4141 }}</ref> About 20% of studies published in ''The American Economic Review'' are contradicted by other studies despite relying on the same or similar data sets.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Goldfarb RS |date=1997-12-01 |title=Now you see it, now you don't: emerging contrary results in economics |journal=Journal of Economic Methodology |volume=4 |issue=2 |pages=221–244 |doi=10.1080/13501789700000016 |issn=1350-178X}}</ref> A study of empirical findings in the ''[[Strategic Management Journal]]'' found that about 30% of 27 retested articles showed statistically insignificant results for previously significant findings, whereas about 4% showed statistically significant results for previously insignificant findings.<ref name="Bergh">{{cite journal | vauthors = Bergh DD, Sharp BM, Aguinis H, Li M |date=6 April 2017 |title=Is there a credibility crisis in strategic management research? Evidence on the reproducibility of study findings |journal=Strategic Organization |volume=15 |issue=3 |pages=423–436 |doi=10.1177/1476127017701076 |s2cid=44024633 |issn=1476-1270 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

==== In water resource management ====
A 2019 study in ''[[Scientific Data (journal)|Scientific Data]]'' estimated with 95% confidence that of 1,989 articles on water resources and management published in 2017, study results might be reproduced for only 0.6% to 6.8%, largely because the articles did not provide sufficient information to allow for replication.<ref name="Stagge_2019">{{cite journal | last1 = Stagge | first1 = JH | last2 = Rosenberg | first2 = DE | last3 = Abdallah| first3= AM | last4 = Akbar | first4 = H | last5 = Attallah | first5 = NA | last6 = James| first6 = R | title = Assessing data availability and research reproducibility in hydrology and water resources | journal = Scientific Data | volume = 6 | article-number = 190030 | date = February 2019 | pmid = 30806638 | pmc = 6390703 | doi = 10.1038/sdata.2019.30 | bibcode = 2019NatSD...690030S }}</ref>

===Across fields===
A 2016 survey by ''Nature'' on 1,576 researchers who took a brief online questionnaire on reproducibility found that more than 70% of researchers have tried and failed to reproduce another scientist's experiment results (including 87% of [[chemist]]s, 77% of [[biologist]]s, 69% of [[physicist]]s and [[engineer]]s, 67% of [[Medicine|medical researchers]], 64% of [[Earth science|earth]] and [[Environmental science|environmental scientists]], and 62% of all others), and more than half have failed to reproduce their own experiments. But fewer than 20% had been contacted by another researcher unable to reproduce their work. The survey found that fewer than 31% of researchers believe that failure to reproduce results means that the original result is probably wrong, although 52% agree that a significant replication crisis exists. Most researchers said they still trust the published literature.<ref name="Nature 5-25-2016" /><ref name="Nature Video_2016">{{cite web |author=Nature Video |date=28 May 2016 |title=Is There a Reproducibility Crisis in Science? |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/video/is-there-a-reproducibility-crisis-in-science/ |access-date=15 August 2019 |website=Scientific American |language=en}}</ref> In 2010, Fanelli (2010)<ref name=Fanelli>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fanelli D | title = "Positive" results increase down the Hierarchy of the Sciences | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 5 | issue = 4 | article-number = e10068 | date = April 2010 | pmid = 20383332 | pmc = 2850928 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0010068 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2010PLoSO...510068F | veditors = Scalas E }}</ref> found that 91.5% of psychiatry/psychology studies confirmed the effects they were looking for, and concluded that the odds of this happening (a positive result) was around five times higher than in fields such as [[astronomy]] or [[geoscience]]s. Fanelli argued that this is because researchers in "softer" sciences have fewer constraints to their conscious and unconscious biases.

Early analysis of [[result-blind peer review]], which is less affected by publication bias, has estimated that 61% of result-blind studies in biomedicine and psychology have led to [[null result]]s, in contrast to an estimated 5% to 20% in earlier research.<ref name="Allen 2019">{{cite journal | vauthors = Allen C, Mehler DM | title = Open science challenges, benefits and tips in early career and beyond | journal = PLOS Biology | volume = 17 | issue = 5 | article-number = e3000246 | date = May 2019 | pmid = 31042704 | pmc = 6513108 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pbio.3000246 | publisher = Public Library of Science | doi-access = free }}</ref>

In 2021, a study conducted by [[University of California, San Diego]] found that papers that cannot be replicated are more likely to be cited.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://rady.ucsd.edu/why/news/2021/05-21-a-new-replication-crisis-research-that-is-less-likely-to-be-true-is-cited-more.html|title=A New Replication Crisis: Research that is Less Likely to be True is Cited More|publisher=[[University of California, San Diego]]|date=2021-05-21|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240413134902/https://rady.ucsd.edu/why/news/2021/05-21-a-new-replication-crisis-research-that-is-less-likely-to-be-true-is-cited-more.html|archive-date=2024-04-13 |url-status=live|access-date=2024-07-20 }}</ref> Nonreplicable publications are often cited more even after a replication study is published.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Serra-Garcia M, Gneezy U | title = Nonreplicable publications are cited more than replicable ones | journal = Science Advances | volume = 7 | issue = 21 | date = May 2021 | article-number = eabd1705 | pmid = 34020944 | pmc = 8139580 | doi = 10.1126/sciadv.abd1705 | bibcode = 2021SciA....7.1705S }}</ref>

== Causes ==
There are many proposed causes for the replication crisis.

===Historical and sociological causes===
The replication crisis may be triggered by the "generation of new data and scientific publications at an unprecedented rate" that leads to "desperation to publish or perish" and failure to adhere to good scientific practice.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Begley CG, Ioannidis JP | title = Reproducibility in science: improving the standard for basic and preclinical research | journal = Circulation Research | volume = 116 | issue = 1 | pages = 116–126 | date = January 2015 | pmid = 25552691 | doi = 10.1161/CIRCRESAHA.114.303819 | s2cid = 3587510 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Predictions of an impending crisis in the quality-control mechanism of science can be traced back several decades. [[Derek de Solla Price]]—considered the father of [[scientometrics]], the [[Quantitative research|quantitative study]] of science—predicted in his 1963 book [[Little Science, Big Science]] that science could reach "senility" as a result of its own exponential growth.<ref name="DeSollaPrice1963">{{cite book | last=De Solla Price |first=Derek J. |url=https://archive.org/details/littlesciencebig0000pric |title=Little Science, Big Science |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=1963 |page=32 |url-access=registration}}</ref> Some present-day literature seems to vindicate this "overflow" prophecy, lamenting the decay in both attention and quality.<ref name="Siebert-al2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Siebert S, Machesky LM, Insall RH | title = Overflow in science and its implications for trust | journal = eLife | volume = 4 | article-number = e10825 | date = September 2015 | pmid = 26365552 | pmc = 4563216 | doi = 10.7554/eLife.10825 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name="Della-Briotta-al2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Della Briotta Parolo P, Pan RK, Ghosh R, Huberman BA, Kaski K, Fortunato S |year=2015 |title=Attention decay in science |journal=Journal of Informetrics |volume=9 |issue=4 |pages=734–745 |arxiv=1503.01881 |bibcode=2015arXiv150301881D |doi=10.1016/j.joi.2015.07.006 |s2cid=10949754}}</ref>

Historian [[Philip Mirowski]] argues that the decline of scientific quality can be connected to its commodification, especially spurred by major corporations' profit-driven decision to outsource their research to universities and [[contract research organization]]s.<ref name="Mirowski 2011">{{cite book | vauthors = Mirowski P |title=Science-Mart |publisher=Harvard University Press |year=2011 |isbn=978-0-674-06113-2 |pages=2, 24}}</ref>

Social [[systems theory]], as expounded in the work of German sociologist [[Niklas Luhmann]], inspires a similar diagnosis. This theory holds that each system, such as economy, science, religion, and media, communicates using its own code: ''true'' and ''false'' for science, ''profit'' and ''loss'' for the economy, ''news'' and ''no-news'' for the media, and so on.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Moeller HG |title=Luhmann explained: from souls to systems |publisher=Open Court |year=2006 |isbn=0-8126-9598-4 |publication-place=Chicago |page=25 |oclc=68694011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Luhmann N |title=Social systems |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1995 |isbn=978-0-8047-2625-2 |publication-place=Stanford, CA |page=288 |oclc=31710315}}</ref> According to some sociologists, science's [[mediatization (media)|mediatization]],<ref name="Scheufele_2014">{{cite journal | vauthors = Scheufele DA | title = Science communication as political communication | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 111 | issue = Suppl 4 | pages = 13585–13592 | date = September 2014 | pmid = 25225389 | pmc = 4183176 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.1317516111 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2014PNAS..111S3585S }}</ref> commodification,<ref name="Mirowski 2011" /> and politicization,<ref name="Scheufele_2014" /><ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Pielke R |title=The honest broker: making sense of science in policy and politics |publisher=Cambridge University Press |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-511-81811-0 |publication-place=Cambridge |page= |doi=10.1017/CBO9780511818110 |oclc=162145073}}</ref> as a result of the structural coupling among systems, have led to a confusion of the original system codes.

=== Problems with the publication system in science ===
==== Publication bias ====
[[Publication bias]]—the tendency to publish only positive, significant results—creates the "[[file drawer effect]]", where negative results remain unpublished.{{efn|In the context of null-hypothesis significance testing, results that are not statistically significant}} This produces misleading literature and biased meta-analyses.<ref name="Romero_2019" /> Only a very small proportion of academic journals in psychology and neurosciences explicitly welcomed submissions of replication studies in their aim and scope or instructions to authors.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Martin GN, Clarke RM | title = Are Psychology Journals Anti-replication? A Snapshot of Editorial Practices | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 8 | page = 523 | date = 2017 | pmid = 28443044 | pmc = 5387793 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2017.00523 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Yeung AW | title = Do Neuroscience Journals Accept Replications? A Survey of Literature | journal = Frontiers in Human Neuroscience | volume = 11 | article-number = 468 | date = 2017 | pmid = 28979201 | pmc = 5611708 | doi = 10.3389/fnhum.2017.00468 | doi-access = free }}</ref> This does not encourage reporting on, or even attempts to perform, replication studies. Among 1,576 researchers ''Nature'' surveyed in 2016, only a minority had ever attempted to publish a replication, and several respondents who had published failed replications noted that editors and reviewers demanded that they play down comparisons with the original studies.<ref name="Nature 5-25-2016" /><ref name="Nature Video_2016" /> An analysis of 4,270 empirical studies in 18 business journals from 1970 to 1991 reported that less than 10% of accounting, economics, and finance articles and 5% of management and marketing articles were replication studies.<ref name="Tsui" /><ref name="Hubbard">{{cite journal |vauthors=Hubbard R, Vetter DE |date=1 February 1996 |title=An empirical comparison of published replication research in accounting, economics, finance, management, and marketing |journal=Journal of Business Research |language=en |volume=35 |issue=2 |pages=153–164 |doi=10.1016/0148-2963(95)00084-4 |issn=0148-2963}}</ref> Publication bias is augmented by the [[Publish or perish|pressure to publish]] and the author's own [[confirmation bias]],{{efn|content=According to the ''APA Dictionary of Psychology'', confirmation bias is "the tendency to gather evidence that confirms preexisting expectations, typically by emphasizing or pursuing supporting evidence while dismissing or failing to seek contradictory evidence".<ref>{{Cite APA Dictionary of Psychology|title=Confirmation bias|access-date=2022-02-02|shortlink=confirmation-bias}}</ref>}} and is an inherent hazard in the field, requiring a certain degree of skepticism on the part of readers.<ref name="Simmons et al. (2011)" />

When publication bias is considered along with the fact that a majority of tested hypotheses might be false ''a priori'', it is plausible that a considerable proportion of research findings might be false positives, as shown by metascientist John Ioannidis.<ref name="Ioannidis2005" /> In turn, a high proportion of false positives in the published literature can explain why many findings are nonreproducible.<ref name="Romero_2019" />

Another publication bias is that studies that do not reject the null hypothesis are scrutinized asymmetrically. For example, they are likely to be rejected as being difficult to interpret or having a Type II error. Studies that do reject the null hypothesis are not likely to be rejected for those reasons.<ref name=":11" />

In popular media, there is another element of publication bias: the desire to make research accessible to the public led to oversimplification and exaggeration of findings, creating unrealistic expectations and amplifying the impact of non-replications. In contrast, null results and failures to replicate tend to go unreported. This explanation may apply to [[power posing]]'s replication crisis.<ref name="NYT_2017_Cuddy">{{cite news | vauthors = Dominus S |date=October 18, 2017 |title=When the Revolution Came for Amy Cuddy |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/18/magazine/when-the-revolution-came-for-amy-cuddy.html |work=New York Times Magazine}}</ref>

==== Mathematical errors ====
Even high-impact journals have a significant fraction of mathematical errors in their use of statistics. For example, 11% of statistical results published in ''Nature'' and ''BMJ'' in 2001 are "incongruent", meaning that the reported p-value is mathematically different from what it should be if it were correctly calculated from the reported test statistic. These errors were likely from typesetting, rounding, and transcription errors.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = García-Berthou E, Alcaraz C | title = Incongruence between test statistics and P values in medical papers | journal = BMC Medical Research Methodology | volume = 4 | issue = 1 | article-number = 13 | date = May 2004 | pmid = 15169550 | pmc = 443510 | doi = 10.1186/1471-2288-4-13 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Among 157 neuroscience papers published in five top-ranking journals that attempt to show that two experimental effects are different, 78 erroneously tested instead for whether one effect is significant while the other is not, and 79 correctly tested for whether their difference is significantly different from 0.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Nieuwenhuis S, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers EJ | title = Erroneous analyses of interactions in neuroscience: a problem of significance | journal = Nature Neuroscience | volume = 14 | issue = 9 | pages = 1105–1107 | date = August 2011 | pmid = 21878926 | doi = 10.1038/nn.2886 }}</ref>

==== "Publish or perish" culture ====
{{further|Publish or perish}}
Academic "publish or perish" culture exacerbates publication bias. Intense pressure to publish in recognized journals, driven by hypercompetitive environments and bibliometric career evaluations, incentivizes researchers to prioritize publishable results over validity.<ref name="Fanelli_2010">{{cite journal | vauthors = Fanelli D | title = Do pressures to publish increase scientists' bias? An empirical support from US States Data | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 5 | issue = 4 | article-number = e10271 | date = April 2010 | pmid = 20422014 | pmc = 2858206 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0010271 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2010PLoSO...510271F }}</ref> According to Fanelli, this pushes scientists to employ a number of strategies aimed at making results "publishable".<ref name="Fanelli_2010a">{{cite journal |last=Fanelli |first=Daniele |title="Positive" Results Increase Down the Hierarchy of the Sciences |journal=PLOS ONE |year=2010 |volume=5 |issue=4 |article-number=e10068 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0010068 |doi-access=free |pmid=20383332 |pmc=2850928 |bibcode=2010PLoSO...510068F }}</ref> In the context of publication bias, this can mean adopting behaviors aimed at making results positive or statistically significant, often at the expense of their validity.<ref name="Fanelli_2010" />

According to Center for Open Science founder Brian Nosek and his colleagues, "publish or perish" culture created a situation whereby the goals and values of single scientists (e.g., publishability) are not aligned with the general goals of science (e.g., pursuing scientific truth). This is detrimental to the validity of published findings.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Nosek BA, Spies JR, Motyl M | title = Scientific Utopia: II. Restructuring Incentives and Practices to Promote Truth Over Publishability | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 615–631 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168121 | pmc = 10540222 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612459058 | arxiv = 1205.4251 | s2cid = 23602412 }}</ref>

Philosopher Brian D. Earp and psychologist Jim A. C. Everett argue that, although replication is in the best interests of academics and researchers as a group, features of academic psychological culture discourage replication by individual researchers. They argue that performing replications can be time-consuming, and take away resources from projects that reflect the researcher's original thinking. They are harder to publish, largely because they are unoriginal, and even when they can be published they are unlikely to be viewed as major contributions to the field. Replications "bring less recognition and reward, including grant money, to their authors".<ref name="Everett 2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Everett JA, Earp BD | title = A tragedy of the (academic) commons: interpreting the replication crisis in psychology as a social dilemma for early-career researchers | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 6 | page = 1152 | date = 2015-01-01 | pmid = 26300832 | pmc = 4527093 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2015.01152 | doi-access = free | author-link2 = Brian Earp }}</ref>

In his 1971 book ''[[Scientific Knowledge and Its Social Problems]]'',<ref name="Ravetz1971">{{cite book |last=Ravetz |first=Jerome R. |title=Scientific Knowledge and Its Social Problems |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford |year=1971}}</ref> philosopher and historian of science [[Jerome R. Ravetz]] predicted that science—in its progression from "little" science composed of isolated communities of researchers to "big" science or "techno-science"—would suffer major problems in its internal system of quality control. He recognized that the incentive structure for modern scientists could become dysfunctional, creating [[perverse incentives]] to publish any findings, however dubious. According to Ravetz, quality in science is maintained only when there is a community of scholars, linked by a set of shared norms and standards, who are willing and able to hold each other accountable.

==== Standards of reporting ====
Certain publishing practices also make it difficult to conduct replications and to monitor the severity of the reproducibility crisis, for articles often come with insufficient descriptions for other scholars to reproduce the study. The Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology showed that of 193 experiments from 53 top papers about cancer published between 2010 and 2012, only 50 experiments from 23 papers have authors who provided enough information for researchers to redo the studies, sometimes with modifications. None of the 193 papers examined had its experimental protocols fully described and replicating 70% of experiments required asking for key reagents.<ref name="Haelle 2021" /><ref name="RP:Cancer" /> The aforementioned study of empirical findings in the ''[[Strategic Management Journal]]'' found that 70% of 88 articles could not be replicated due to a lack of sufficient information for data or procedures.<ref name="Tsui" /><ref name="Bergh" />  In [[water resources]] and [[Water resource management|management]], most of 1,987 articles published in 2017 were not replicable because of a lack of available information shared online.<ref name="Stagge_2019" /> In studies of [[event-related potential]]s, only two-thirds the information needed to replicate a study were reported in a sample of 150 studies, highlighting that there are substantial gaps in reporting.<ref name=":4">{{cite journal | vauthors = Clayson PE, Carbine KA, Baldwin SA, Larson MJ | title = Methodological reporting behavior, sample sizes, and statistical power in studies of event-related potentials: Barriers to reproducibility and replicability | journal = Psychophysiology | volume = 56 | issue = 11 | article-number = e13437 | date = November 2019 | pmid = 31322285 | doi = 10.1111/psyp.13437 | url = https://psyarxiv.com/kgv9z/ }}</ref>

==== Procedural bias ====
By the [[Duhem–Quine thesis|Duhem-Quine thesis]], scientific results are interpreted by both a substantive theory and a theory of instruments. For example, astronomical observations depend both on the theory of astronomical objects and the theory of telescopes.
