Once the method and analysis plan is vetted through peer-review, publication of the findings is provisionally guaranteed, based on whether the authors follow the proposed protocol. One goal of registered reports is to circumvent the [[publication bias]] toward significant findings that can lead to implementation of questionable research practices. Another is to encourage publication of studies with rigorous methods.

The journal ''[[Psychological Science (journal)|Psychological Science]]'' has encouraged the [[preregistration]] of studies and the reporting of effect sizes and confidence intervals.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lindsay DS | title = Replication in Psychological Science | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 26 | issue = 12 | pages = 1827–1832 | date = December 2015 | pmid = 26553013 | doi = 10.1177/0956797615616374 | doi-access = free }}</ref> The editor in chief also noted that the editorial staff will be asking for replication of studies with surprising findings from examinations using small sample sizes before allowing the manuscripts to be published.

==== Metadata and digital tools for tracking replications ====
It has been suggested that "a simple way to check how often studies have been repeated, and whether or not the original findings are confirmed" is needed.<ref name="2021-05-replication-crisis"/> Categorizations and ratings of reproducibility at the study or results level, as well as addition of links to and rating of third-party confirmations, could be conducted by the peer-reviewers, the scientific journal, or by readers in combination with novel digital platforms or tools.

=== Statistical reform ===

==== Requiring smaller ''p''-values ====
Many publications require a [[P-value|''p''-value]] of ''p''&nbsp;&lt;&nbsp;0.05 to claim [[statistical significance]]. The paper "Redefine statistical significance",<ref name="redefine">{{cite journal | vauthors = Benjamin DJ, Berger JO, Johannesson M, Nosek BA, Wagenmakers EJ, Berk R, Bollen KA, Brembs B, Brown L, Camerer C, Cesarini D, Chambers CD, Clyde M, Cook TD, De Boeck P, Dienes Z, Dreber A, Easwaran K, Efferson C, Fehr E, Fidler F, Field AP, Forster M, George EI, Gonzalez R, Goodman S, Green E, Green DP, Greenwald AG, Hadfield JD, Hedges LV, Held L, Hua Ho T, Hoijtink H, Hruschka DJ, Imai K, Imbens G, Ioannidis JP, Jeon M, Jones JH, Kirchler M, Laibson D, List J, Little R, Lupia A, Machery E, Maxwell SE, McCarthy M, Moore DA, Morgan SL, Munafó M, Nakagawa S, Nyhan B, Parker TH, Pericchi L, Perugini M, Rouder J, Rousseau J, Savalei V, Schönbrodt FD, Sellke T, Sinclair B, Tingley D, Van Zandt T, Vazire S, Watts DJ, Winship C, Wolpert RL, Xie Y, Young C, Zinman J, Johnson VE | title = Redefine statistical significance | journal = Nature Human Behaviour | volume = 2 | issue = 1 | pages = 6–10 | date = January 2018 | pmid = 30980045 | doi = 10.1038/s41562-017-0189-z | hdl-access = free | doi-access = free | hdl = 10281/184094 }}<!-- Previously available as a preprint at: {{Cite journal | doi=10.31234/osf.io/mky9j | title=Redefine statistical significance| vauthors = Benjamin DJ, Berger J |year = 2017}} --></ref> signed by a large number of scientists and mathematicians, proposes that in "fields where the threshold for defining statistical significance for new discoveries is ''p''&nbsp;&lt;&nbsp;0.05, we propose a change to ''p''&nbsp;&lt;&nbsp;0.005. This simple step would immediately improve the reproducibility of scientific research in many fields." Their rationale is that "a leading cause of non-reproducibility (is that the) statistical standards of evidence for claiming new discoveries in many fields of science are simply too low. Associating 'statistically significant' findings with ''p''&nbsp;&lt;&nbsp;0.05 results in a high rate of false positives even in the absence of other experimental, procedural and reporting problems."<ref name="redefine" />

This call was subsequently criticised by another large group, who argued that "redefining" the threshold would not fix current problems, would lead to some new ones, and that in the end, all thresholds needed to be justified case-by-case instead of following general conventions.<ref>{{Cite journal| vauthors = Lakens D, Adolfi FG, Albers CJ, Anvari F, Apps MA, Argamon SE, Baguley T, Becker RB, Benning SD, Bradford DE, Buchanan EM |date=March 2018|title=Justify your alpha|journal=Nature Human Behaviour|language=en|volume=2|issue=3|pages=168–171|doi=10.1038/s41562-018-0311-x|issn=2397-3374|hdl-access=free|hdl=21.11116/0000-0004-9413-F|s2cid=3692182| url = https://pure.eur.nl/en/publications/a51f8b58-977c-45cc-a977-f1c5839e5afe }}</ref> A 2022 followup study examined these competing recommendations' practical impact. Despite high citation rates of both proposals, researchers found limited implementation of either the p < 0.005 threshold or the case-by-case justification approach in practice. This revealed what the authors called a "vicious cycle", in which scientists reject recommendations because they are not standard practice, while the recommendations fail to become standard practice because few scientists adopt them.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Białek M, Misiak M, Dziekan M | title = The vicious cycle that stalls statistical revolution | journal = Nature Human Behaviour | date = 2022 | volume = 7 | issue = 2 | pages = 161–163 | doi = 10.1038/s41562-022-01515-3 | pmid = 36690817 }}</ref>

==== Addressing misinterpretation of ''p''-values ====
{{Further|Misuse of p-values}}
Although statisticians are unanimous that the use of "''p''&nbsp;&lt;&nbsp;0.05" as a standard for significance provides weaker evidence than is generally appreciated, there is a lack of unanimity about what should be done about it. Some have advocated that [[Bayesian methods]] should replace ''p''-values. This has not happened on a wide scale, partly because it is complicated and partly because many users distrust the specification of prior distributions in the absence of hard data. A simplified version of the Bayesian argument, based on testing a point null hypothesis was suggested by pharmacologist [[David Colquhoun]].<ref name=DC2014>{{cite journal | vauthors = Colquhoun D | title = An investigation of the false discovery rate and the misinterpretation of p-values | journal = Royal Society Open Science | volume = 1 | issue = 3 | article-number = 140216 | date = November 2014 | pmid = 26064558 | pmc = 4448847 | doi = 10.1098/rsos.140216  | doi-access = free| arxiv = 1407.5296 | bibcode = 2014RSOS....140216C }}</ref><ref name="DC2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Colquhoun D | title = The reproducibility of research and the misinterpretation of ''p''-values | journal = Royal Society Open Science | volume = 4 | issue = 12 | article-number = 171085 | date = December 2017 | pmid = 29308247 | pmc = 5750014 | doi = 10.1098/rsos.171085  | doi-access = free| bibcode = 2017RSOS....471085C }}</ref> The logical problems of inductive inference were discussed in "The Problem with p-values" (2016).<ref name="DC2016">{{cite magazine | vauthors = Colquhoun D |title=The problem with p-values |url=https://aeon.co/essays/it-s-time-for-science-to-abandon-the-term-statistically-significant |magazine=Aeon Magazine |access-date=11 December 2016 | date = 11 October 2016 }}</ref>

The hazards of reliance on ''p''-values arises partly because even an observation of ''p''&nbsp;=&nbsp;0.001 is not necessarily strong evidence against the null hypothesis.<ref name="DC2017"/> Despite the fact that the likelihood ratio in favor of the alternative hypothesis over the null is close to 100, if the hypothesis was implausible, with a prior probability of a real effect being&nbsp;0.1, even the observation of ''p''&nbsp;=&nbsp;0.001 would have a false positive risk of 8 percent. It would still fail to reach the 5 percent level.

It was recommended that the terms "significant" and "non-significant" should not be used.<ref name="DC2017"/> ''p''-values and confidence intervals should still be specified, but they should be accompanied by an indication of the false-positive risk. It was suggested that the best way to do this is to calculate the prior probability that would be necessary to believe in order to achieve a false positive risk of a certain level, such as 5%. The calculations can be done with various computer software.<ref name="DC2017"/><ref name="fpr-calc">{{cite web | vauthors = Longstaff C, Colquhoun D |title=Calculator for false positive risk (FPR) |url=http://fpr-calc.ucl.ac.uk/ |publisher=University College London | id = version 1.7}}</ref> This reverse Bayesian approach, which physicist [[Robert Matthews (scientist)|Robert Matthews]] suggested in 2001,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Matthews RA |title=Why should clinicians care about Bayesian methods? |journal=Journal of Statistical Planning and Inference |date=2001 |volume=94 |pages=43–58 |doi=10.1016/S0378-3758(00)00232-9 }}</ref> is one way to avoid the problem that the prior probability is rarely known.

==== Encouraging larger sample sizes ====
To improve the quality of replications, larger [[Sample size determination|sample sizes]] than those used in the original study are often needed.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Maxwell SE, Lau MY, Howard GS | title = Is psychology suffering from a replication crisis? What does "failure to replicate" really mean? | journal = The American Psychologist | volume = 70 | issue = 6 | pages = 487–498 | date = September 2015 | pmid = 26348332 | doi = 10.1037/a0039400 }}</ref> Larger sample sizes are needed because estimates of [[effect size]]s in published work are often exaggerated due to publication bias and large sampling variability associated with small sample sizes in an original study.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = IntHout J, Ioannidis JP, Borm GF, Goeman JJ | title = Small studies are more heterogeneous than large ones: a meta-meta-analysis | journal = Journal of Clinical Epidemiology | volume = 68 | issue = 8 | pages = 860–869 | date = August 2015 | pmid = 25959635 | doi = 10.1016/j.jclinepi.2015.03.017 | hdl-access = free | doi-access = free | hdl = 2066/153978 }}</ref><ref name="Button 365–376">{{cite journal | vauthors = Button KS, Ioannidis JP, Mokrysz C, Nosek BA, Flint J, Robinson ES, Munafò MR | title = Power failure: why small sample size undermines the reliability of neuroscience | journal = Nature Reviews. Neuroscience | volume = 14 | issue = 5 | pages = 365–376 | date = May 2013 | pmid = 23571845 | doi = 10.1038/nrn3475 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |title = Consequences of prejudice against the null hypothesis |url = https://faculty.washington.edu/agg/pdf/Gwald_PsychBull_1975.OCR.pdf |journal = Psychological Bulletin |pages = 1–20 |volume = 82 |issue = 1 |doi = 10.1037/h0076157 |language = en-US | vauthors = Greenwald AG |year = 1975}}</ref> Further, using [[Statistical significance|significance thresholds]] usually leads to inflated effects, because particularly with small sample sizes, only the largest effects will become significant.<ref name=":7">{{cite journal | vauthors = Amrhein V, Korner-Nievergelt F, Roth T | title = The earth is flat (''p'' > 0.05): significance thresholds and the crisis of unreplicable research | journal = PeerJ | volume = 5 | article-number = e3544 | date = 2017 | pmid = 28698825 | pmc = 5502092 | doi = 10.7717/peerj.3544 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==== Cross-validation ====
One common statistical problem is [[overfitting]], that is, when researchers fit a regression model over a large number of variables but a small number of data points. For example, a typical fMRI study of emotion, personality, and social cognition has fewer than 100 subjects, but each subject has 10,000 voxels. The study would fit a sparse linear regression model that uses the voxels to predict a variable of interest, such as self-reported stress. But the study would then report on the p-value of the model ''on the same data'' it was fitted to. The standard approach in statistics, where data is split into a [[Training, validation, and test data sets|training and a validation set]], is resisted because test subjects are expensive to acquire.<ref name=":12" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kriegeskorte N, Simmons WK, Bellgowan PS, Baker CI | title = Circular analysis in systems neuroscience: the dangers of double dipping | journal = Nature Neuroscience | volume = 12 | issue = 5 | pages = 535–540 | date = May 2009 | pmid = 19396166 | doi = 10.1038/nn.2303 | pmc = 2841687 }}</ref>

One possible solution is [[Cross-validation (statistics)|cross-validation]], which allows model validation while also allowing the whole dataset to be used for model-fitting.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Yarkoni T, Westfall J | title = Choosing Prediction Over Explanation in Psychology: Lessons From Machine Learning | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 12 | issue = 6 | pages = 1100–1122 | date = November 2017 | pmid = 28841086 | pmc = 6603289 | doi = 10.1177/1745691617693393 }}</ref>

=== Replication efforts ===

==== Funding ====
In July 2016, the [[Netherlands Organisation for Scientific Research]] made €3 million available for replication studies. The funding is for replication based on reanalysis of existing data and replication by collecting and analysing new data. Funding is available in the areas of social sciences, health research and healthcare innovation.<ref>{{cite press release|title=NWO makes 3 million available for Replication Studies pilot |url=http://www.nwo.nl/en/news-and-events/news/2016/nwo-makes-3-million-available-for-replication-studies-pilot.html |website=Netherlands Organisation for Scientific Research |date= July 2016 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160722134546/http://www.nwo.nl/en/news-and-events/news/2016/nwo-makes-3-million-available-for-replication-studies-pilot.html | archive-date= 22 July 2016 | url-status= deviated }}</ref>

In 2013, the [[Laura and John Arnold Foundation]] funded the launch of [[Reproducibility Project|The Center for Open Science]] with a $5.25 million grant. By 2017, it provided an additional $10 million in funding.<ref name=Wired2017>{{cite news | vauthors = Apple S |title=The Young Billionaire Behind the War on Bad Science |url=https://www.wired.com/2017/01/john-arnold-waging-war-on-bad-science/|magazine=Wired |date=January 22, 2017}}</ref> It also funded the launch of the [[Meta-Research Innovation Center at Stanford]] at Stanford University run by Ioannidis and medical scientist [[Steven Goldman (scientist)|Steven Goodman]] to study ways to improve scientific research.<ref name=Wired2017/> It also provided funding for the [[AllTrials]] initiative led in part by medical scientist [[Ben Goldacre]].<ref name=Wired2017/>

==== Emphasis in post-secondary education ====
{{See also|Project-based learning}}
Based on coursework in experimental methods at MIT, Stanford, and the [[University of Washington]], it has been suggested that methods courses in psychology and other fields should emphasize replication attempts rather than original studies.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Frank MC, Saxe R | title = Teaching Replication | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 600–604 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168118 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612460686 | s2cid = 33661604 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Grahe JE, Reifman A, Hermann AD, Walker M, Oleson KC, Nario-Redmond M, Wiebe RP | title = Harnessing the Undiscovered Resource of Student Research Projects | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 605–607 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168119 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612459057 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Marwick B, Wang L, Robinson R, Loiselle H |title=How to Use Replication Assignments for Teaching Integrity in Empirical Archaeology |journal=Advances in Archaeological Practice |volume=8 |date=22 October 2019 |pages=78–86 |doi=10.1017/aap.2019.38 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Such an approach would help students learn scientific methodology and provide numerous independent replications of meaningful scientific findings that would test the replicability of scientific findings. Some have recommended that graduate students should be required to publish a high-quality replication attempt on a topic related to their doctoral research prior to graduation.<ref name="Everett 1152">{{cite journal | vauthors = Everett JA, Earp BD | title = A tragedy of the (academic) commons: interpreting the replication crisis in psychology as a social dilemma for early-career researchers | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 6 | page = 1152 | date = 2015-01-01 | pmid = 26300832 | pmc = 4527093 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2015.01152 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==== Replication database ====
There has been a concern that replication attempts have been growing.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ziano |first1=Ignazio |last2=Mok |first2=Pui Yan (Cora) |last3=Feldman |first3=Gilad |date=August 2021 |title=Replication and Extension of Alicke (1985) Better-Than-Average Effect for Desirable and Controllable Traits |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1948550620948973 |journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science |language=en |volume=12 |issue=6 |pages=1005–1017 |doi=10.1177/1948550620948973 |issn=1948-5506}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Pennington |first=Charlotte R. |title=A student's guide to open science: using the replication crisis to reform psychology |date=2023 |publisher=Open University Press |isbn=978-0-335-25117-9 |location=Maidenhead}}</ref> As a result, this may lead to lead to research waste.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kulke |first1=Louisa |last2=Rakoczy |first2=Hannes |date=2018-02-01 |title=Implicit Theory of Mind – An overview of current replications and non-replications |journal=Data in Brief |volume=16 |pages=101–104 |doi=10.1016/j.dib.2017.11.016 |issn=2352-3409 |pmc=5694957 |pmid=29188228|bibcode=2018DIB....16..101K }}</ref> In turn, this has led to a need to systematically track replication attempts. As a result, several databases have been created (e.g.<ref name=":18">{{Cite web |title=Curate Science |url=https://curatescience.org/app/home/#about |access-date=2024-09-19 |website=curatescience.org}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=LeBel |first1=Etienne P. |last2=McCarthy |first2=Randy J. |last3=Earp |first3=Brian D. |last4=Elson |first4=Malte |last5=Vanpaemel |first5=Wolf |date=September 2018 |title=A Unified Framework to Quantify the Credibility of Scientific Findings |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/2515245918787489 |journal=Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science |language=en |volume=1 |issue=3 |pages=389–402 |doi=10.1177/2515245918787489 |issn=2515-2459}}</ref>). The databases have created a replication database that includes psychology among other disciplines, to promote theory-driven research and optimize the use of academic and institutional resource, while promoting trust in science.<ref name=":18" />

===== Final year thesis =====
Some institutions require [[undergraduate]] students to submit a final year thesis that consists of an original piece of research. Daniel Quintana, a psychologist at the University of Oslo in Norway, has recommended that students should be encouraged to perform replication studies in thesis projects, as well as being taught about [[open science]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Quintana DS | title = Replication studies for undergraduate theses to improve science and education | journal = Nature Human Behaviour | volume = 5 | issue = 9 | pages = 1117–1118 | date = September 2021 | pmid = 34493847 | doi = 10.1038/s41562-021-01192-8 | type = World View article | s2cid = 237439956 }}</ref>

===== Semi-automated =====
[[File:Semi-automated testing of reproducibility and robustness of the cancer biology literature by robot.jpg|thumb|400px|"The overall process of testing the reproducibility and robustness of the cancer biology literature by robot. First, text mining is used to extract statements about the effect of drugs on gene expression in breast cancer. Then two different teams semi-automatically tested these statements using two different protocols, and two different cell lines (MCF7 and MDA-MB-231) using the laboratory automation system Eve."]]
Researchers demonstrated a way of semi-automated testing for reproducibility: statements about experimental results were extracted from, as of 2022 [[Semantic Web|non-semantic]], gene expression cancer research papers and subsequently reproduced via [[Laboratory robotics#Applications|robot scientist]] "[[Robot Scientist#Adam and Eve|Eve]]".<ref>{{cite news |title='Robot scientist' Eve finds that less than one-third of scientific results are reproducible |url=https://techxplore.com/news/2022-04-robot-scientist-eve-one-third-scientific.html |access-date=15 May 2022 |author=((University of Cambridge)) | work = Techxplore | date = April 2022 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Roper K, Abdel-Rehim A, Hubbard S, Carpenter M, Rzhetsky A, Soldatova L, King RD | title = Testing the reproducibility and robustness of the cancer biology literature by robot | journal = Journal of the Royal Society, Interface | volume = 19 | issue = 189 | article-number = 20210821 | date = April 2022 | pmid = 35382578 | pmc = 8984295 | doi = 10.1098/rsif.2021.0821 }}</ref> Problems of this approach include that it may not be feasible for many areas of research and that sufficient experimental data may not get extracted from some or many papers even if available.

==== Involving original authors ====
Psychologist [[Daniel Kahneman]] argued that, in psychology, the original authors should be involved in the replication effort because the published methods are often too vague.<ref name="Chambers">{{cite news | vauthors = Chambers C |date=10 June 2014 |title=Physics envy: Do 'hard' sciences hold the solution to the replication crisis in psychology? |newspaper=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/science/head-quarters/2014/jun/10/physics-envy-do-hard-sciences-hold-the-solution-to-the-replication-crisis-in-psychology}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Kahneman D |year=2014 |title=A New Etiquette for Replication |url=<!-- alternative url https://www.scribd.com/document/225285909/Kahneman-Commentary --> |journal=Social Psychology |volume=45 |issue=4 |pages=310–311 |doi=10.1027/1864-9335/a000202 |doi-access= |series=Commentaries and Rejoinder on<!-- this is correct, there is no other word after "on" --> |type=Commentary }}</ref> Others, such as psychologist Andrew Wilson, disagree, arguing that the original authors should write down the methods in detail.<ref name="Chambers" /> An investigation of replication rates in psychology in 2012 indicated higher success rates of replication in replication studies when there was author overlap with the original authors of a study<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Makel MC, Plucker JA, Hegarty B | title = Replications in Psychology Research: How Often Do They Really Occur? | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 537–542 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 26168110 | doi = 10.1177/1745691612460688 | doi-access = free }}</ref> (91.7% successful replication rates in studies with author overlap compared to 64.6% successful replication rates without author overlap).

==== Big team science ====
The replication crisis has led to the formation and development of various large-scale and collaborative communities to pool their resources to address a single question across cultures, countries and disciplines.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Uhlmann EL, Ebersole CR, Chartier CR, Errington TM, Kidwell MC, Lai CK, McCarthy RJ, Riegelman A, Silberzahn R, Nosek BA | title = Scientific Utopia III: Crowdsourcing Science | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 14 | issue = 5 | pages = 711–733 | date = September 2019 | pmid = 31260639 | doi = 10.1177/1745691619850561 | doi-access = free }}</ref> The focus is on replication, to ensure that the effect generalizes beyond a specific culture and investigate whether the effect is replicable and genuine.<ref name=":3">{{cite journal | vauthors = Forscher PS, Wagenmakers EJ, Coles NA, Silan MA, Dutra N, Basnight-Brown D, IJzerman H | title = The Benefits, Barriers, and Risks of Big-Team Science | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 18 | issue = 3 | pages = 607–623 | date = May 2023 | pmid = 36190899 | doi = 10.1177/17456916221082970 | s2cid = 236816530 | url = https://psyarxiv.com/2mdxh/ }}</ref> This allows interdisciplinary internal reviews, multiple perspectives, uniform protocols across labs, and recruiting larger and more diverse samples.<ref name=":3" /> Researchers can collaborate by coordinating data collection or fund data collection by researchers who may not have access to the funds, allowing larger sample sizes and increasing the robustness of the conclusions.

=== Broader changes to scientific approach ===

==== Emphasize triangulation, not just replication ====
Psychologist [[Marcus Munafo|Marcus R. Munafò]] and Epidemiologist [[George Davey Smith]] argue, in a piece published by ''[[Nature (journal)|Nature]]'', that research should emphasize [[Triangulation (social science)|triangulation]], not just replication, to protect against flawed ideas. They claim that,

{{blockquote|replication alone will get us only so far (and) might actually make matters worse&nbsp;... [Triangulation] is the strategic use of multiple approaches to address one question. Each approach has its own unrelated assumptions, strengths and weaknesses. Results that agree across different methodologies are less likely to be [[Artifact (error)|artefacts]].&nbsp;... Maybe one reason replication has captured so much interest is the often-repeated idea that [[Falsifiability|falsification]] is at the heart of the scientific enterprise. This idea was popularized by [[Karl Popper]]'s 1950s maxim that theories can never be proved, only falsified. Yet an overemphasis on repeating experiments could provide an unfounded sense of certainty about findings that rely on a single approach.&nbsp;... philosophers of science have moved on since Popper. Better descriptions of how scientists actually work include what epistemologist [[Peter Lipton]] called in 1991 "[[inference to the best explanation]]".<ref name=Munafo2018>{{cite journal | vauthors = Munafò MR, Davey Smith G | title = Robust research needs many lines of evidence | journal = Nature | volume = 553 | issue = 7689 | pages = 399–401 | date = January 2018 | pmid = 29368721 | doi = 10.1038/d41586-018-01023-3 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2018Natur.553..399M }}</ref>}}

==== Complex systems paradigm ====
{{Further|Complex system}}
The dominant scientific and statistical model of causation is the linear model.<ref name="Wallot 2018" /> The linear model assumes that mental variables are stable properties which are independent of each other. In other words, these variables are not expected to influence each other. Instead, the model assumes that the variables will have an independent, linear effect on observable outcomes.<ref name="Wallot 2018">{{Cite journal | vauthors = Wallot S, Kelty-Stephen DG |date=2018-06-01 |title=Interaction-Dominant Causation in Mind and Brain, and Its Implication for Questions of Generalization and Replication |journal=Minds and Machines |language=en |volume=28 |issue=2 |pages=353–374 |doi=10.1007/s11023-017-9455-0 |issn=1572-8641 |doi-access=free|hdl=21.11116/0000-0001-AC9C-E |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

Social scientists Sebastian Wallot and Damian Kelty-Stephen argue that the linear model is not always appropriate.<ref name="Wallot 2018" /> An alternative is the complex system model which assumes that mental variables are interdependent. These variables are not assumed to be stable, rather they will interact and adapt to each specific context.<ref name="Wallot 2018" /> They argue that the complex system model is often more appropriate in psychology, and that the use of the linear model when the complex system model is more appropriate will result in failed replications.<ref name="Wallot 2018" />

{{blockquote|text=...psychology may be hoping for replications in the very measurements and under the very conditions where a growing body of psychological evidence explicitly discourages predicting replication. Failures to replicate may be plainly baked into the potentially incomplete, but broadly sweeping failure of human behavior to conform to the standard of independen[ce] ...<ref name="Wallot 2018" />}}

==== Replication should seek to revise theories ====
Replication is fundamental for scientific progress to confirm original findings. However, replication alone is not sufficient to resolve the replication crisis. Replication efforts should seek not just to support or question the original findings, but also to replace them with revised, stronger theories with greater explanatory power. This approach therefore involves pruning existing theories, comparing all the alternative theories, and making replication efforts more generative and engaged in theory-building.<ref>{{Cite journal  | vauthors = Tierney W, Hardy JH, Ebersole CR, Leavitt K, Viganola D, Clemente EG, Gordon M, Dreber A, Johannesson M, Pfeiffer T, Uhlmann EL |date=2020-11-01 |title=Creative destruction in science  |journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes |language=en |volume=161 |pages=291–309 |doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2020.07.002 |s2cid=224979451 |issn=0749-5978|doi-access=free |hdl=2066/228242 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Tierney W, Hardy J, Ebersole CR, Viganola D, Clemente EG, Gordon M, Hoogeveen S, Haaf J, Dreber A, Johannesson M, Pfeiffer T |date=2021-03-01 |title=A creative destruction approach to replication: Implicit work and sex morality across cultures |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |language=en |volume=93 |article-number=104060 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2020.104060 |s2cid=229028797 |issn=0022-1031|doi-access=free |hdl=10037/24275 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> However, replication alone is not enough, it is important to assess the extent that results generalise across geographical, historical and social contexts is important for several scientific fields, especially practitioners and policy makers to make analyses in order to guide important strategic decisions. Reproducible and replicable findings was the best predictor of generalisability beyond historical and geographical contexts, indicating that for social sciences, results from a certain time period and place can meaningfully drive as to what is universally present in individuals.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Delios A, Clemente EG, Wu T, Tan H, Wang Y, Gordon M, Viganola D, Chen Z, Dreber A, Johannesson M, Pfeiffer T, Uhlmann EL | title = Examining the generalizability of research findings from archival data | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 119 | issue = 30 | article-number = e2120377119 | date = July 2022 | pmid = 35858443 | pmc = 9335312 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.2120377119 | first13 = Eric Luis | first14 = Ahmad M. | first15 = Ajay T. | bibcode = 2022PNAS..11920377D | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==== Open science ====
{{Further|Open science}}
[[File:Tenets of open science.svg|thumb|alt=Six coloured hexagons with text on them are arranged around the words "Tenets of Open Science". Starting at the top right and moving clockwise, the text on the hexagons says: Reproducibility of results; Scientific integrity; Citizen science; Promotion of collaborative work; Ease of access to knowledge for all; and Stimulation of innovation. Underneath the hexagons, there is a large exclamation point, and text saying "Plus: better citation rates for open access articles and research data".|Tenets of [[open science]]]]
Open data, open source software and open source hardware all are critical to enabling reproducibility in the sense of validation of the original data analysis. The use of proprietary software, the lack of the publication of analysis software and the lack of open data prevents the replication of studies. Unless software used in research is open source, reproducing results with different software and hardware configurations is impossible.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ince DC, Hatton L, Graham-Cumming J | title = The case for open computer programs | journal = Nature | volume = 482 | issue = 7386 | pages = 485–488 | date = February 2012 | pmid = 22358837 | doi = 10.1038/nature10836 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2012Natur.482..485I }}</ref> [[CERN]] has both Open Data and CERN Analysis Preservation projects for storing data, all relevant information, and all software and tools needed to preserve an analysis at the large experiments of the [[LHC]]. Aside from all software and data, preserved analysis assets include metadata that enable understanding of the analysis workflow, related software, systematic uncertainties, statistics procedures and meaningful ways to search for the analysis, as well as references to publications and to backup material.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Vuong QH | title = The (ir)rational consideration of the cost of science in transition economies | journal = Nature Human Behaviour | volume = 2 | issue = 1 | page = 5 | date = January 2018 | pmid = 30980055 | doi = 10.1038/s41562-017-0281-4 | s2cid = 46878093 | doi-access = free }}</ref> CERN software is open source and available for use outside of [[particle physics]] and there is some guidance provided to other fields on the broad approaches and strategies used for open science in contemporary particle physics.<ref>{{Cite journal |title = Reproducibility and Replication of Experimental Particle Physics Results |journal = Harvard Data Science Review |date = 2020-12-21 |volume = 2 |issue = 4 |doi = 10.1162/99608f92.250f995b | vauthors = Junk TR, Lyons L |arxiv = 2009.06864 |bibcode = 2020HDSRv...2f995bJ |s2cid = 221703733}}</ref>

Online repositories where data, protocols, and findings can be stored and evaluated by the public seek to improve the integrity and reproducibility of research. Examples of such repositories include the [[Open Science Framework]], [[Registry of Research Data Repositories]], and Psychfiledrawer.org. Sites like Open Science Framework offer badges for using open science practices in an effort to incentivize scientists. However, there have been concerns that those who are most likely to provide their data and code for analyses are the researchers that are likely the most sophisticated.<ref name="Ioannidis 2016">{{cite journal | vauthors = Ioannidis JP | title = Anticipating consequences of sharing raw data and code and of awarding badges for sharing | journal = Journal of Clinical Epidemiology | volume = 70 | pages = 258–260 | date = February 2016 | pmid = 26163123 | doi = 10.1016/j.jclinepi.2015.04.015 | doi-access =  | type = Commentary<!-- cite journal is still the correct template, it's a commentary article published in a journal --> }}</ref> Ioannidis suggested that "the paradox may arise that the most meticulous and sophisticated and method-savvy and careful researchers may become more susceptible to criticism and reputation attacks by reanalyzers who hunt for errors, no matter how negligible these errors are".<ref name="Ioannidis 2016" />

== Further reading ==
{{refbegin|30em}}
* {{cite web | vauthors = Bastian H |title=Reproducibility Crisis Timeline: Milestones in Tackling Research Reliability |url=https://blogs.plos.org/absolutely-maybe/2016/12/05/reproducibility-crisis-timeline-milestones-in-tackling-research-reliability/ |website=Absolutely Maybe |access-date=5 June 2019 |date=5 December 2016}}
* Bonett, D.G. (2021). Design and analysis of replication studies. Organizational Research Methods, 24, 513–529. https://doi.org/10.1177/1094428120911088
* {{cite magazine| vauthors = Denworth L |date=October 2019|url=http://verso.mat.uam.es/~pablo.fernandez/Fisherb.pdf|title=A Significant Problem: Standard scientific methods are under fire. Will anything change?|magazine=[[Scientific American]]|volume=321|issue=4|pages=62–67|quote=The use of [[p value|''p'' values]] for nearly a century [since 1925] to determine [[statistical significance]] of [[experiment]]al results has contributed to an illusion of [[certainty]] and [to] [[reproducibility|reproducibility crises]] in many [[science|scientific fields]]. There is growing determination to reform statistical analysis... Some [researchers] suggest changing statistical methods, whereas others would do away with a threshold for defining 'significant' results.|quote-page=63}}
* {{cite book | vauthors = Harris R |title=Rigor Mortis: How Sloppy Science Creates Worthless Cures, Crushes Hope, and Wastes Billions |date=2017 |publisher=Basic Books |location=New York |isbn=978-0-465-09790-6}}
* {{cite journal | vauthors = Kafkafi N, Agassi J, Chesler EJ, Crabbe JC, Crusio WE, Eilam D, Gerlai R, Golani I, Gomez-Marin A, Heller R, Iraqi F, Jaljuli I, Karp NA, Morgan H, Nicholson G, Pfaff DW, Richter SH, Stark PB, Stiedl O, Stodden V, Tarantino LM, Tucci V, Valdar W, Williams RW, Würbel H, Benjamini Y | title = Reproducibility and replicability of rodent phenotyping in preclinical studies | journal = Neuroscience and Biobehavioral Reviews | volume = 87 | pages = 218–232 | date = April 2018 | pmid = 29357292 | pmc = 6071910 | doi = 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2018.01.003 | author-link16 = Donald W. Pfaff | author-link26 = Yoav Benjamini | author-link4 = John C. Crabbe | author-link7 = Robert Gerlai | author-link5 = Wim Crusio }}
* {{cite book | vauthors = Ritchie S |author-link1=Stuart J. Ritchie |title=Science Fictions: How Fraud, Bias, Negligence, and Hype Undermine the Search for Truth |date=July 2020 |publisher=Metropolitan Books |location=New York |isbn=978-1-250-22269-5}} [https://www.theamericanconservative.com/articles/the-crisis-wrecking-modern-science/ Book Review] (November 2020, ''[[The American Conservative]]'')
* {{cite journal | vauthors = Whitfield J | title = Replication Crisis | journal = [[London Review of Books]] | volume = 43 | issue = 19 | date = October 2021 | pages = 39–40 | url = https://www.lrb.co.uk/the-paper/v43/n19/john-whitfield/replication-crisis }} review of {{cite book | vauthors = Ritchie S | author-link1 = Stuart J. Ritchie |title=Science Fictions: Exposing Fraud, Negligence and Hype in Science |date= July 2020 |location=London |isbn=978-1-84792-565-7 | publisher = Bodley Head }}
{{refend}}
